review_text: Died after 5 months of use.  This toothbrush (my wife's) was not even close to the quality of my Sonicare toothbrush, which was much more expensive (and worth the price).  Caveat emptor!
The tooth brush head is so tiny and soft.  It may be good for a child's baby teeth but not for anyone else.  I wish I could send it back but I actually tried it once.

I thought I was getting a super deal on this toothbrush - not true.My hubby's Sonicare toothbrush was $100 and I thought this was the same but at a great price.  Unfortunately, this one is super loud.  Not pleased at all.

I am also surprised Sonicare is still making this model.  I have owned Sonicares for years, probably bought 6-7 of them and my whole family has them.  There have been several generations of Sonicare, and up until the most recent generation, they had two critical flaws in my book, and this model shares those flaws:  (1) they only work on 110v - so if you travel outside the Americas, you have to carry a heavy converter (not adapter, voltage converter) or else they burn out and (2) the brush head is poorly designed so that water and saliva goes down into a reservoir inside which becomes a gunky stinky mess.  The entry level new design corrects both of these problems and is available on Amazon for about $10 more than this one -- and well worth it.  It's called the Sonicare Easy Clean.Philips Sonicare HX6511/50 Easy Clean Rechargeable Electric ToothbrushHope this helps... if you order this brush, just know about the two flaws above... other wise, like all Sonicares, it provides excellent cleaning and you'll love using it.  If price is your main consideration over all else, this is the model... otherwise spend a little bit more for the Easy Clean.Hope this helps!

This toothbrush is too big for female hand, the way it vibrate is very annoying, its too strong, and you actually can't brush your teeth with your mouth open.I suggest you look at other more durable brands..

This toothbrush is not sanitary.  Standing water and spit collect inside the brush.  You can see this when you remove the head.  The manual recommends cleaning inside the brush once a week!.  I stopped using the brush because I believe it can cause health problems.  I contacted customer service more than 5 times and they have not gotten back to me. They just give me the same line - that someone will get back to me in 2 days.  I'll take the loss of $60 for the toothbrush and $25 for extra heads.  I'll never purchase a Philips product again.

OMG! The other reviewer was right: this thing is like a jack-hammer. My gums are still buzzing a half-hour later. I've owned a lot of electric toothbrushes and this one is dangerous. I had to count my teeth after using it. Get something else. You could not possibly do worse.

Updated review with more detail to explain my 1 star opinion---As I've shared in previous reviews for Oral-B electric toothbrush products, one of the Oral-B "regular" electric toothbrushes (similar to this ORAL-B PROFESSIONAL 1000) that I bought a few years ago made my teeth sensitive to the point of having pain. My dentist, who had recommended the Oral-B toothbrush in the first place, diagnosed the Oral-B electric toothbrush as being to blame for taking the enamel off my teeth. This was a temporary condition that resolved on its own once I discontinued its use per the dentist's order. At that time under the dentist's direction, I went back to using a Sonicare electric toothbrush. However not much later, Oral-B came out with their first "SmartSeries" which has a special sensor that monitors if the user is pressing too hard on the teeth--this is indicated by reading the signals on an LCD display. My dentist then recommended the SMARTSERIES model.My husband and two children have been using theOral-B Professional Care SmartSeries 5000 Rechargeable Toothbrushfor a few years.  We own two units because my kids have their own bathroom. They all like the SMARTSERIES 5000. I like that it helps teach my children just how much pressure is enough and when it is too much. The dentist and I feel this helps prevent damage to their enamel due to erroneous use of too much pressure.The ORAL-B 1000 PROFESSIONAL CARE electric toothbrush product which I tested for Amazon Vine claims to stop pulsing if too much pressure is put on the teeth. I cannot detect any difference in action no matter how hard I push against my teeth. I also tried pushing it against a fingertip and the palm of my hand.In reading the directions in the box I got confused because the manufacturer is using one set of directions for multiple models of their toothbrushes but not being specific on the directions as to which model is in the box. I then turned to the box for a closer reading of exactly what the 1000 series could do. I found small writing on the back and one side to indicate this model has one mode brushing which they say is for daily use. In the direction packet it states other models have two modes, one for "daily use" and one for sensitive teeth that has less pressure. There is also a notation that some models have a tooth whitening feature (no model numbers are stated or I'd share them here).This model comes with one regular brush head. By close reading of the direction packet I learned there is another model of head that is for sensitive teeth.If you are shopping for an electric toothbrush you need to know there are many options on the market, even with this latest series by Oral-B there are different models, some with more options than others.Even though my dentist has never given me a diagnosis of having "sensitive teeth" I don't like the sensation of this 1000 series Oral-B toothbrush, it is very strong and since it doesn't seem to stop any action no matter how much pressure I exert on it, I worry it is too rough on my teeth. I plan to stick to using my Sonicare. My husband and kids are happily still using their SMART SERIES with an LCD panel that tells them if they are pressing too hard.

The saying "you get what you pay for" applies. I've used Oral B electric toothbrushes for years. I liked the small footprint of the charger, plus a $20 rebate! How could I lose? This is truly a piece of junk. It rattles, it vibrates like a jack hammer, it even sounds cheap and like it's going to fall apart. Yes, I did get my rebate, unlike several people who reviewed this. The worst 2 things, tho, are that water somehow gets in the brush head and, unless you take it off and rinse out the inside, it starts smelling like swamp water. It's not just the head that came with the toothbrush. I've replaced the head, and the same thing happens. Pretty gross. I have to take the brush off every couple of days and soak it in Listerine antibiotic mouthwash. So apparently the unit doesn't seal to the brush somehow, despite the little o-rings that come with the brush heads. The second thing is that this wonderful small footprint charger get very hot. I'm very careful to dry the entire toothbrush every time I use it, but within a couple of days, a brown burnt gunk starts forming on the charger surface (the part that the toothbrush sits on) and every day there's more until I scrape it off and start over again. I think I'll just order another one and try to shame Oral B into a refund. This hot charger and burnt gunk make it hazardous, I think. Next time, I'll get the one I usually get. Pay the money and forget the cheap.

Don't fall for the $20 rebate scam. You buy the brush you mail everything in then instead of $20 then send you a rebate of $10 with no explanation or anything, when we called they said they would look into it then would call us back, never did. They also said we might have used a coupon with it and they don't allow both we dont remember using a coupon when buying it and they now have the original receipt so you can't check it or can't return it without the UPC. Pretty convenient for them huh? I don't even want the brush now since they basically stole my money to get me to buy it.I want my $10 back. Oral B I hope this review costs you more than my $10 you stole. I also might contact the FTC.As far as the brush goes it suppoed to occulate and vibrate it only occulates and it's very rough. I like my old battery operated one better, this is not worth the money.

We purchased two of these, and from the first use on, both would start and stop. This was not a feature, as it happened intermittently. Why this happened I have no idea, as the brush seemed to be powerful. But this did not bode well for an appropriate lifespan, so we returned them.

I bought this toothbrush, new, out of the box. It short circuited at least once every time I used it for three weeks. It was returned and a new brush was sent out immediately. The new brush is worse. It stops and starts several times during brushing. Don't believe we will buy oral B products again.

I bought this and was surprised when my teeth hurt after just one brushing. I tried it out for a few more days and they got worse. I had to stop using it. I sent it back as it was guaranteed, but never got my money back. If you want to try it, get it at a store so you can take it back if you don't like it.

This has happened with the past 2 sonicares I've purchased. They last for a few months but then end up losing their charge, and stop vibrating the brush thistles as much as is necessary to be useful. I'm returning to non-electric toothbrushes because these are just too expensive. Perhaps there's a higher quality alternative to Philips on the market... not sure

I'm not going to cover the merits of using a Sonicare electric toothbrush.  Just use them.  Plain and simple.  You will save more in dental costs than you will spend on them.  I have been using them for probably over 15 years, and they are must-haves.  Everyone in my family uses them.  I have also used every major generation of Sonicare toothbrush and several models within each.This Essence 5600 is styled like the original first generation.  Unlike the first generation which used NiCad batteries, this uses NiMH batteries which was used in the second generation.  The original first generation toothbrushes used an open sort of toothbrush head with a plate behind the brush.  This was actually a good head, although it rattled/buzzed if you made the little plastic guard/plate touch the moving brush head.  No big deal.  With these you occasionally had to unscrew the head and clean them out (with an old-style toothbrush).However, Philips/Sonicare has discontinued the original style head and gone with a "universal" head that fits both the first and second generation toothbrushes.  That is what you get with this model.  Unfortunately, despite the more effective and more comfortable head, these are plagued with the problem of mold, bacteria, and just general funk under the rubber seal.  The rubber seal just doesn't seal at all.  Moisture gets in the heads, and shortly, there will be massive black and pink hairy things growing inside.  Just truly disgusting which is why you see some aftermarket (non-Philips) seals being sold.  Further, it is just next to impossible to clean these second generation heads.  See my remedy at the end.The third generation (current) is truly the best.  They went with Li-Ion batteries.  The handles are smaller.  The heads no longer have a "compartment" in which to grow mold and bacteria.Unfortunately, with the progression of each model, Sonicare keeps adding more features which I don't like.  I find quad pacer useless and annoying.  All of the additional massage and pulsing modes and what-not are just irritating because it makes such a simple device much harder to use.  For example, instead of a simple on/off, you now have to push the button twice or cycle through these modes.  This is important because if you push a button twice to cycle it and turn it off while it is in the middle of changing modes, instead of it turning off, it turns on, and your spit and toothpaste go flying, so now you have to clean your glasses, the mirror, and pretty much everything within 3 feet.Anyway, sorry to digress, but I have UNIVERSALLY found that the best model with in each generation is the most simple one without all the feature-laden whistles and bells, ridiculous UV cleaners, etc., which just drive up your cost both initial and in consumables.So, in sum, this has good battery technology, but you will have to be careful not to leave it in the charger and make sure you run it down.  In other words, you will have to run it like a usual Ni-MH battery product to get the maximum life of maybe 3-5 years.  If you don't, then you can kill it is 1-2 years.  I like that this is a simple version and easy to operate.  It has a nice grip and travel pouch.  It it also is relatively inexpensive if you are new to Sonicare, but your next one will want to be the Li-Ion versions.  What I really don't like about this is the terrible toothbrush heads which get funky.  My best recommendation for dealing with this is as follows.  (Disclaimer: this is not recommended or supported by the manufacturer.  Use at your own risk, yada, yada.)  Get some small, sharp scissors or a box cutter or utility knife and cut off that rubber seal -- which doesn't seal anyway.  Then, after you brush your teeth just run some water down in there to rinse it out.  Without the seal, it will dry, and not get so funky.  At least 10X better, although it looks kinda MacGyver'ed.
This model did not have the timing beeps. I ordered another model that has beeps every 30 seconds.  So it is just a back up.  Cory
Water seeps through the plastic handle where it joins.  This leaves goop and yuck that requires frequent cleaning.  Surely the new design toothbrushes seal better than this one.

I have just about ran out of patience with Philips & "quality" after owning 5 of this brand in the past.This time with the new "Sonicare" on which the charging light stays on 24/7 after 2 weeks of ownership. It is supposed to be on ONLY WHEN THE BATTERY IS BEING CHARGED. (So says Phillips)Have checked the new charger that came with the unit. It appears fine. Tried the charger from the old unit. It also appears fine.Ever tried to get a reply from Philips customer service on the Net?I no longer would recommend this brand even though I will continue to use until it (or I) die.Not expected from Phillips.John

I jumped at the opportunity to try out the Sonicare HX5610/01 electric toothbrush and after using it for about a month I have mixed feelings on the product.First, this toothbrush is really designed for a single person or for an individual who travels. This isn't really a practical toothbrush for more than an individual simply because there's no place to store an extra toothbrush head. I'm coming from using an Oral B electric toothbrush that had slots for storing more than one brush-head and was immediately disappointed that there isn't a similar feature here.Second this is a very basic electric toothbrush. For $70 you're getting a single function electric toothbrush, simply meaning that there's only an on/off button and no variable speed brushing or vibrating. There's a built in 2-minute timer that's the norm for electric toothbrushes; a charger; and a small storage bag. That's absolutely it. The toothbrush itself seems to be either poorly designed or just an early design that's never been improved upon. The major problem that I see with this brush is that water and toothpaste trickle down the handle and get inside the housing. After using this brush for a few days it left a mess of old toothpaste on the charger and my bathroom counter. Not only is this gross but I can't imagine toothpaste and water trickling down the inner components of this brush being very good for longevity.Lastly I was floored that this new Sonicare is not only heavier and bulkier than my 6 year old Oral B electric toothbrush but also considerably louder. Brushing with this thing is like standing on a subway platform as a train flies by; in other words a jarring experience.I would definitely not recommend this product. Even as an entry level electric toothbrush this is a hard sell given the price. Spend a bit more and upgrade to a better Sonicare or Oral B.

I have been using Sonicare electric toothbrushes for more than 10 years and like them for brushing my teeth and gums. Every model I have purchased including two of this model series has had a convenient feature whereby every 30 seconds the brush motor would pause and I would shift to another section of my mouth.Now some greedy executives at Phillips have decided to disable this function on some toothbrush submodels like this HX5610/30 so they can upcharge for it on their standard models. This is now called by Phillips "quadpacer" and you now need to pay extra for this basic functionality.Phillips also makes a recharging base that is 110v AC only, one that is sold in Europe that is 220v AC only, and one that is 110/220v AC which can be used anywhere in the world with only a plug adapter. I had to buy the 110/220v charger base directly from Phillips for an extra $45 plus shipping. Phillips should provide this dual voltage base as the standard charger or at least make it readily available through the same merchants that sell their toothbrushes.My next electric toothbrush will not be a Sonicare. I can pay less and get more with other brands which have gotten better reviews from testers.

Not sure what happened. It was fine one day and the next day it started vibrating so fast and so hard it literally hurt my mouth. Amazon has a very conservative return policy, so I am SOOO glad I did not buy a more expensive product. I would never buy another unless it was from a store where I  could return it if this happened again.I cannot even use it now. Big waste of money.

This electric toothbrush does seem to deliver a better tooth cleaning than just using a manual brush.  However, it had some limitations and awkwardness that make me much prefer the Sonic-care, which I have used for years.  I did not find the round and round motion of the brush particularly comfortable.  The tube that the brush head is attached to is too thick, so that it often hit against the top of my teeth if I was brushing the bottoms or vice versa.  The motor is not terribly strong--when the brush hit the teeth on  the opposite side, the brush head slowed or stopped completely.  It also had a tendency to stop when it was turned in the vertical position --i.e. brushing the biting surfaces of the upper teeth.  This model allegedly had three different brush speeds for persons of different sensitivity--I could never get them to work.  It always ran at the same speed no matter what I did with the on button.  Finally, this brush has no timing or reminder feature.  On the Sonic-care, there is a two minute timer and it beeps every 30 seconds which allows you to divide your mouth into four quadrants and give each equal time.  And also to make sure that you brush for the whole recommended two minutes.  I really missed this feature on the Oral-B.  If I were shopping for an electric toothbrush this would not be the one I would buy or recommend to friends.

So I have to say I really liked this brush...it replaced an older Oral-B that was simply worn out after 4 years of use. I liked the upgrades they made the multi modes and the pressure light are excellent additions.So then there is the problem. Two problems actually. First off the unit gets warm when it is on the charger. And not just when it is "charging", even when the unit shows full charge if you leave it on the stand it still heats up.  I find this very disconcerting in a home type consumer appliance.  I tried only charging it when needed. This leads us to issue 2. It does not seem to have much of a reserve. I find even with a full charge it only lasts about 3 days (6 uses) and then needs to be topped off again. There is a noticeable drop off in speed and torque when brushing as the charge drops.  So what to say, this could be a great unit...but with the heat issue and the weak batteries I think it's just not worth the price point.  I tried to contact the company to see if this heat issue may be a sign there is a battery charging issue and hence leading to a weak reserve.  But I have not received a response.

Having had great experiences with the Philips Sonicare toothbrushes, specifically Elite models HX7300 and HX7800, I ordered this item from the Amazon Vine program expecting an improvement.Alas, I must agree with others that this brush leaves several things to be desired. Firstly, in addition to easily trapping toothpaste and moisture inside the handle, where the brush screws in, this new handle has added another design flaw --- a seam in the handle that will obviously also attract moisture and toothpaste.Additionally, the 30-second reminder signal to switch tooth surfaces is now omitted --- or, rather, missing in action. That was a critical feature to ensure that one brushes an equal time on each surface.Alas, the latest Sonicare is not the great item I'd have expected from Philips and, sadly, I cannot recommend this model.

This sonicare works wonders on my teeth, almost to just from the dentist feel. My only gripe is if the pastic part of the brush touches your other teeth it feels like a jackhammer in your mouth. Ratta tat tat! I'd think they would have a softer buffer on the neck of the brush, to prevent such an uncomfortable sensation.Also, it has a cheap feeling base, I am wondering how long it will last, it feels cheap, which is a shame because the body of the brush (the fat part you hold) feels much more sturdy. The brush end also feels very cheap. So, the base at the bottom where the recharger is located and the top end, the brushing end, are much less of a quality product than the base mechanism, which houses the rechargeable battery and the mechanical parts.The travel bag is nice, and this brush stayed charged for four days, it may have gone longer but I forgot when I got home and just plopped it back in the recharger/holder.I will do an update in the future, as I often do for those interested in longevity. I've used it roughly two weeks now.For those wondering, it is made in China.

I don't quite get what Philips is doing with the release of this new toothbrush. I have used Sonicare toothbrushes for many years and to me, this reminds me of my very first Sonicare brush - very basic, heavy and wide handle, and worse of all that screw on head that is so famous for retaining water which quickly turns to mold if not rigoursly cleaned out after each use.I'll stick with Sonicare for sure, but prefer my Healthy White model that is slimmer, more light weight, and with the head that easily comes off and doesn't trap moisture.

Anything Phillips and anything Sonicare is reliable in its market group. This one is thick and heavy, be prepared for this property. On the otherhand it is a thorough item and competes well in the electric toothbrush offerings because it is the one to go with if you drop these alot in the bathroom.Jay Adler Music
It holds a charge longer then a week of use which is a plus.  My last model was a little more advanced.  This one is a good basic unit.

This electric toothbrush is Sonicare's lowest level model.  It has the basic features that come with every Sonicare, even the cheapest 39.99 one.  I have owned a Sonicare for over 10 years and have had 3 different models.  Can I tell the difference between the 3 models I have owned....not really.  I brush my teeth for the 2 minutes and any other features usually don't get used on a daily basis when I am rushing to get to work or to go out with friends.  The reason why I take two stars away from this toothbrush is because of a flaw in the style of this toothbrush.  This particular brush allows for water, toothpaste and oral fluids to get inside of the toothbrush.  The toothbrush mechanism goes over the body of the handle and twists off.  Water and other fluids get inside of the toothbrush causing it to rust and malfunction if you do not clean it out often.  Because of this flaw which is the exact same style as my original Sonicare, I could not wait to upgrade to thePhilips Sonicare HX6711/02 HealthyWhite 710 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushin 2010.  It was gross to have to keep cleaning the inside of the toothbrush mechanism.  If you look at the pictures of each of these toothbrushes, you will see that this one twists over the handle while the one I bought in 2010 is all one piece and you don't have to worry about oral fluids getting into the toothbrush.  For 5 dollars more you can get the HX6711 HealthyWhite toothbrush which I feel is far superior to this toothbrush.  The travel case is nice but, I would rather put my toothbrush head in a zip-lock bag for travel then buy this toothbrush just for the case.  Not having to deal with cleaning the inner casing of the toothbrush in order to keep the toothbrush working properly is not worth the price in my opinion.  If you want an electric toothbrush with the simple features such as the 2 minute timer....get the lower priced model on Amazon.  Why this toothbrush is 70 dollars, I have no idea.  It looks and feels like the 40 dollar brush that Amazon sells.

The toothbrush is fine.  Does what you expect from a Sonicare.  But $39 (what I paid) and now $45 (the current price) is all just too much for this very basic model.  Buy it somewhere else or wait for a deal.

This was a great deal on a toothbrush but I had bought so many things that I was forced to send it back because I didn't have a use for it.  Shipping was fast but I now have to pay to ship it back (consequences of a wrong decision) oh well, I learned a lesson!

Bought this to replace one we'd had for many years, and still worked, but was falling apart.  First time I used it I realized it doesn't have the 30 second incremental beeps.  I thought they all had that, but apparently not.  Otherwise it's fine, just not what I wanted.

This is the "no bells and whistles" version of the Sonicare.  It is very serviceable and I have few gripes about it.  I guess my main gripe is that eventually crud gets into the cavity inside the replaceable brush modules and gets kind of gross.  It would be nice if Philips would figure out a way to prevent this; it seems to me to be a trait of questionable hygienics.  Other than that, this is a simple, robust little device.  RJB.
its Ok, Its not fun to use, I am not sure as its my first electric brush but its just sitting there and not using any more.

We got this to replace our old Braun Sonicare which was difficult to keep clean.Although this product is comparatively easier to keep clean, it's messier to use.The brush heads are a hollow tube with holes at the top and the bottom, so when you brush, even if you keep your mouth shut, you'll dribble spit and toothpaste out of the bottom of the brush head.That's fine if you brush leaning over the sink, but if you don't, you're going to be wiping up drips from your feet or the floor.The other issue with this brush is that my dentist told me it can be harder on the gums than Sonicare.

I hate the vibrating feeling in my mouth. I was forced to buy this when Interplak stropped making their rotating toothbrushes. I LOVED Interplak. I HATE the sonic vibrating style of any brush but there's nothing else now. It doesn't clean as well as the spinning brushes despite their claim to. This is just OK. Please come back with a spinning bristle head!

well truthfully, i bought this because i didnt want to drop double the $ on sonicare--i figured test drive this baby and then if i feel like its worth it, i will consider the investment in sonicare. well about a few weeks in, i can say that i still like regular brushing over this. i tend to stay away from battery operated anything and a toothbrush is no exception here. i just prefer the feel and weight of an ergonomic toothbrush. i get the kids sized and make sure its super soft and i just go at it--with this ive found there is a lot of thought involved, its just not as routine yet.

Oral-B was offering a 50% rebate on all of its power toothbrushes so I figured this was a great chance to try out an alternative to the Sonicare toothbrush I had been using. Even without the 50% rebate the cost of the Oral-B models are lower than the Sonicare models. After doing quite a bit of research I also found that the only differences between the Professional Care 1000 and higher models like the 4000 and 5000 are the number of brush modes and unnecessary add-ons like timers and displays on the toothbrush itself. If your teeth are used to a good scrubbing there is no need to even use anything other than the standard brush mode.After about a week of use I found myself really missing the Sonicare technology. I didn't feel like the spinning bristles of the Oral-B were cleaning my teeth as well as the vibrating/pulsing bristles of the Sonicare.Oral-B used to make a sonic toothbrush called the Sonic Complete which still remains the best toothbrush I've owned. Unfortunately these spinning bristle models don't really do it for me and if you're used to sonic technology you will likely be as disappointed as I was.

It's not just the noise, which you'll get used to eventually.  But if you don't have a big mouth or some special skill which I seem to lack, the machine will CATCH ON YOUR LIPS or even your tongue.  I admit that I haven't actually been WOUNDED yet, but it is certainly disconcerting, and the first couple times it might scare you, so be warned.

This is at least my third Oral-b electric toothbrush, the first was a AA battery powered model that wore out mainly from a lack of waterproofing. The second had rechargeable batteries and ran for around $20 in 2003. It did well until the on off switch wore out.The first thing I noticed in Oral-b 100 Professional Care 1000 Electric toothbrush is the battery/ charge indicator. It lets you know when the toothbrush is charging, is charged, and needs charged. Depending on your daily use, it can go about a week with the low battery red light comes on.The brush head technology hasn't noticeably changed ]during these years. It does a good job of getting around and between teeth, though the small opening in the plastic casing means, saliva, toothpaste and water will all get in their, which can get gross, unless you rinse well and take the brush head off from time to time to rinse out the inside. I'm sure there is a reason for this hole, but I think it should go.Like most things, it comes down to personal tastes, but I found myPhilips Sonicare R732 Healthy White Power Toothbrushdoes a better job of getting my teeth clean and has three speed settings. It does of course cost twice as much, but comes with a travel case and two brush heads.Overall, this Oral-B model is acceptable, though I question why it's so much better than some of its less expensive counterparts simply because it has a battery indicator and the word Professional in its name.
I believe this is a an older model.  There is one that works just as well thats about ten dollars just has one less speed.  I would invest a little more to get the better model.

This is a great brush when it is working. Every now and then, it stops for a second then starts again. It is not from brushing too hard. It happens even if the brush is running out of my mouth. I must have received a faulty unit. While it is working, it cleans really well and I am overall happy with it.

I got this toothbrush because it was a good price with a rebate from a local big box store.Pros:-rechargeable-cleans well-interchangeable heads-comes with standCons:-stand gets dirty very easily and has to be cleaned constantly-toothbrush base gets dirty from stand and has to be cleaned constantly-replacement heads are relatively expensive (in relation to cost of toothbrush)Overall, I would recommend it as a cheap rechargeable toothbrush, but I would caution you to look for one with a better stand/toothbrush system that doesnt hold the water so much. It creates a little water bath that quickly turns colors and smells bad. I have to clean it daily which defeats the point of having a stand. I think it should be angled so the water drains out.

This is my first ever electric toothbrush, and I guess I'm just not super impressed. It's a little unwieldy (especially the stand it sits on), and mine takes FOREVER to charge (which may just be a problem with mine). I do like the clean it gives the teeth, but I don't necessarily believe it's anything a toothbrush can't do (except for maybe the clean I get around the gum line). I haven't been using it that long...after a month or two I'll try and update. If you're an electric toothbrush connoisseur well, then, you probably know more than me about this sort of thing. If this would be your first venture into the electric toothbrush market I would say..."Do you really need it, is there something else you need more or want more? Then pass on this stick with your good old manual toothbrush." For the person who is just dead set on experiencing what the electric toothbrush can bring to their lives I would say "Spend no more money what this one costs...it's more than good enough."

I've used a SoniCare electric toothbrush for several years.  It is more expensive, but after using the Oral-B I'm convinced it is well worth it.The Oral-B is loud...the kind of loud that makes me wonder if the motor is doing okay.  The brush head that it comes with is thick...and I have to open up more than is comfortable to get to my teeth.  If I happen to bite down on it at all...mostly because it just doesn't fit in my mouth well...the motor bogs down easily to the point of stopping completely.  I've never had this happen with my SoniCare.The Oral-B handle is slimmer than the SoniCare...and I like that...it has multiple heads for different purposes that can be purchased (all are round and spin though) and a decent case to store them in right on the charger.  That is handy.  There is no cover on the head...so it is exposed to anything that flies by while on the charger.  I like the brush covers on the SoniCare and that the heads are smaller and the traditional toothbrush shape.I ended up giving the Oral-B to my daughter, who is loving it since it is much nicer than the $5 electric toothbrushes she's had until now.  She doesn't seem to mind opening so wide.  I will be surprised if the Oral-B motor/battery lasts several years the way the SoniCare does.

I've only owned SONICARE toothbrushes for the past five years, which are heavier than the Oral B 1000. Oral B is lower in price and seems to do an effective job. Great thing about Oral B is it's very lightweight, easily packed for travel and offers a large variety of replacement head brushes. The brush itself was harder than I'd like but they do offer softer ones for those with sensitive teeth and for those with braces, implants, crowns and bridges.  Overall, a reasonably priced product, lightweight. The battery is built in, so you will need to recharge accordingly, most likely two or three times a month. I wish the brush heads came with covers like Sonicare.

Update 1/12/11. I've had this for some time now, and in many ways was preferring it to my Sonicare as noted below. However, the unit starting pooping out quickly even after 24 hour charges. Silly me, I didn't realize that unlike the Sonicare this was also requires a battery. A battery that should be no big deal to pop out and replace like most any other battery in the world right?No.The instructions with my unit are impossible to follow and whatever print is on the bottom of the unit impossible to read. Outside of taking it into the office and let the lads diddle with it (and they do have better things to do), I googled for instructions and found a video for battery replacement. Watched the video, but my charger unit doesn't have the *key* in the back/bottom to open the battery case and no matter how hard I twist it isn't opening. Worse yet, the old brush replacement head doesn't twist off too easily either.I guess if you're willing to put up with agonizing every time you need to change a battery or the brush you've got a fairly good product. Me, this is going in the drawer and I'm going back to my Sonicare which in all these years has never ever asked me for a new battery.And I didn't think I'd ever say that. I've used a Sonicare for years, as I have deeper pockets and need to keep things as clean as possible, so I was thrilled to have the chance to get an Oral B and compare the two. Yes, the round brush is smaller than the Sonicare one, but I found that a big plus in one area I have difficulty keeping clean - that being the back inside of the lower front where the orthodontist put a permanent wire to keep the lower teeth straight. That smaller head, along with the revolving motion really gets in there quite nicely and I'll be curious to see if the hygienist notices a different at the next cleaning.Another plus is the Oral B shuts off if you put too much pressure whilst brushing (you'll wear at your gums if you do that). One caveat with this model, there is only the single stand/charger and if you're going to purchase one for several users (swapping out individual brush heads of course) I would recommend finding another Oral B model with a stand that would hold them all for you.

I had never used an electric toothbrush prior to receiving the Oral-B. I always wondered if the results were worth the expense -- after all, I can get a free toothbrush twice a year from my dentist when I get my teeth cleaned, or I can spend $5.95 for a pack of four brushes at any big box store. After using this rechargeable brush, I think a plain old non-electric tooth brush does just fine. For one thing, it takes hours and hours to charge the Oral-B for the first time, for another there is no cover for the brush when it is recharging, and third I find the time allowed to brush to be a little short for me. That was another thing: I thought I could brush as long as I wanted with an electric toothbrush, just as with my regular brush. The motor also seemed loud to me, but maybe some people would not be bothered by that. My teeth felt clean, but no cleaner than with a manual brush. To buy or not to buy I think is up to the individual -- if you like an electric toothbrush over a manual one, this is probably just as good a choice as any.

First the good things. It works. In fact, I found the battery life to be outstanding. I took the toothbrush on a 12 day trip and it lasted that long despite only being rated for 10 days. It cleans your teeth pretty well. I also like the pressure indicator that lets you know if you're pushing too hard. Because it's fully functional, I don't feel like I can give it less than 3 stars. But here's why it doesn't get more.First, the brush cycle. I've used other electric toothbrushes that also have the concept of a brush cycle, a beep, series of beeps, difference in the brush, whatever, that informs you to switch to a different area of your mouth. This toothbrush has that too and it works. Then it keeps working. The others I've used ran through the full cycle then turned off. This one, just kept going. You have to manually switch it off before it will stop. It's not a big deal since I know what part of my teeth I'm brushing, but it's part of the general  behavior that I don't like.Next, drool. Again, I've used other tooth brushes and I know that you have to maintain a good seal or you'll spray things everywhere and/or drool all over your chest. But with this brush, I was getting drool despite maintaining a good seal. I was very careful and always was leaking. Why exactly? I'm not sure, but I attribute it somehow to the shape or action or ergonomics.Finally, the easiest to fix, I think the brush is way to strong. If I were to continue using this, I'd need a softer brush.It might just be me, but I'm just not crazy about it even though it works adequately.

I've had this Oral-B Smartseries 4000 Professional Care electric toothbrush since June, 2012.  The charge has never lasted more than 10-11 uses of two minutes apiece.  The owner's manual claims that a full charge will give you 20 uses of two minutes each use.  That's two times what I actually get.  AND, it takes in excess of 13 hours for the brush to fully charge.  I say 13+ hours because I set it on the charge cord when I get the red low charge light, and the elapsed time I observed that the re-charge lights are still flashing is 13 hours.  Full charge occurred some time after that during the night.  The 3 green re-charging lights go off after a full charge.The brush head that comes with this unit has odd little paddles and different bristles, and is a little rough on my gums, so I use a spare one I had for my 10+ year-old Oral-B electric toothbrush.  I don't really use any of the fancy additional settings, just the basic 2-minute, and it cleans very well.  I only changed out because the rechargeable  battery on the old one was finally giving out, and wasn't accessible to replace.  WARNING, if you open the battery compartment before the warranty is up, the instruction booklet states, "Caution! Opening the handle will destroy the appliance and invalidate the warranty."  As little charge as I get on this, I wonder if it will last for the two years?Three stars for the sucky battery life and lack of ability to replace without destroying the unit.  But it does clean my teeth very well!

I have had several Oral B power toothbrushes.  This one works well but the design is a bit annoying.First, the stand.  Instead of the previous models which have a small footprint with a little tower behind the brush/unit to hold extra brushes, this has a tray that extends over a wider footprint.  It isn't covered and it takes up more room than it needs to.As for the brush, my main annoyance is with the control of modes.  There are several modes (timed, polish, sensitive).  There is a button that you push under the "on" button that cycles through these.  You can only do this once you have turned the brush on... and then the indicator lights are pointed at you with paste on it!  You can't even see in the mirror what setting it is at (like you can for the pressure alarm light).  I am always thinking I have it on the wrong setting.  This is an annoying and poor design.Otherwise, it is fine.

My mother in law bought the whole family sonicare tooth brushes right after they came out.  This is now my third version of the tooth brush.  They seem to last me about 3 year a piece.  There really doesn't seem like there is much different about each version.  The head are a bit smaller than they used to be.  The power is about the same.  The batteries seem to last longer.  (I forgot my charger on vacation and it only died the last morning of a week of vacation.  And I am pretty sure it wasn't fully charged to start with.)The main feature I really like about this particular model is completely ascetic.  The handle has blue strives up the side.  Since my wife and I both have sonicare tooth brushes it is sometimes hard to tell the difference (just a small circle of color on the bottom of the head.)  But now that mine has a blue handle I won't ever accidentally grab the wrong one.  I really do think these are great tooth brushes and the price really has come down from where they were originally.The only issue i that they do need to be cleaned every once in a while.  I am not sure this is a design issue, but tooth paste seems to get into the handle a little more than the first generation ones did.  But this one is a bit better than the last one that I had in that problem.

This is the bare bones, no frills version of the Sonicare, without any of the bells and whistles of the more expensive models.There is no travel charger, no UV cleaning feature, no extra heads or color-code rings, and no audible signal or vibration for the 30 second change-over time.Regardless, it still does an excellent job on your teeth, which is the most important function after all.  It comes in an easy to open box, with a travel case and base charger, and is your basic one-man (or woman) tooth cleaning machine.An improvement would be if they could include those little tongue-cleaning nodule-thingies on the back of the brush head.Bottom line is that it's basic, but it does the job.Amanda Richards, February 3, 2013

The Phillips Sonicare line of products is an effective way of brushing teeth and gums. The concept is about having vibration help in the brushing process and as long as the user follows the instructions it works wonderfully. However, it takes a set amount of time to use this effectively. If used in a rush-type manner you will not see the results you're looking for.I was actually pretty skeptical when my dentist recommended it. It wasn't that my brushing was bad but like a lot of people I was missing spots at times. When I eventually tried it I made sure to follow the instructions provided along with suggestions from the dentist. First, make use of the timer provided. The brush runs two minutes and then shuts off. Use the brush two minutes for the top and two minutes for the bottom. Second, make sure your angle your brush against the teeth in a way that works the gum line as well. This is where a lot of normal brushing misses. Third, after brushing use the massage setting along with mouthwash to finish off with a clean mouth. You do this by dipping the brush head into the mouthwash and massaging your gums for about a minute. All told, it takes about 5-6 minutes to complete the process. Do this everyday and your next check up will be amazing.I would highly recommend this as a superior way to promote dental hygiene. As long as you take your time and be diligent you will have great results.

The was the first sonicare I owned before I upgraded to the healthy white model. This is the cheapest one, but that doesn't mean that it lacks in cleaning power in any way. The vibrations took a little bit of getting used to, but after awhile, I actually started to like it. One thing I love it is the fact that after I was done brushing, it feels after-a-dentist-cleaning clean :) It gets the spaces inbetween your teeth and even under the gumline. It says on the box that you get better dental checkups, and that is the truth!The only drawbacks to this model is the size and how the brush head is designed. The size is a little bulky compared to the other models, but it's not horrible. Also the brush head is a pretty big assembly that screws onto the top. Water and toothpaste can get under this assembly so you have to periodically take the top off and clean under the brush head and the place where it screws onto.All-in-all, if you want a sonicare, but don't want to burn a huge hole in your wallet, this is a nice one to start out with.
As advertised good product cleans my teeth well but does not have the beep function. other than that great product.

I've owned 2 Sonicare brushes.  This is the most basic unit that I've used.  They all have provided a great clean feeling and really make a huge difference from a regular toothbrush.  I have not noticed a bit of compromise in the brushing between this and the more deluxe versions that I've owned in the past.  It holds a great charge for I've taken it on several trips and not had to recharge after several days of usage.  Two points that I do not like about this brush:  It does not have any 30 second timer like the more upgraded units have to notify you to change brushing locations.  I had always found that a nice feature to make sure you get an even cleaning. It is also much larger than the more expensive models.  If you plan to travel a lot with it, you might want to look into a different model.  With those two aside, it is a great, inexpensive toothbrush and does what it's suppose to really well.

Overall, the Sonicare Essence 5600 is an excellent basic electric toothbrush.  The Essence 5600 just has an on/off switch and a 2-minute timer.  In my opinion, these features are sufficient for most people.  Other models have slightly more features.  For example, I use a Sonicare HealthyWhite 3 Mode toothbrush, which has a setting for Clean, Sensitive, and Clean and White.  You can actually hear and feel the difference at the different settings, e.g., the sensitive setting doesn't vibrate as vigorously as the clean setting.  The vibration of the Essence 5600 is like the vibration on the "Clean" setting on my toothbrush.There are two things I don't like about the Essence 5600.  First, it costs more to replace the brush heads.  A 3-pack of brush heads for my HealthyWhite is cheaper than a 2-pack of brush heads for the Essence 5600.Second, the Essence 5600 is not compact relative to other electric toothbrushes.  (1) The Essence 5600 is just big in almost every respect especially compared to my HealthyWhite.  First, the handle itself is much larger in diameter.  (2) The brush head is also big (as it includes the dome shaped connector to the body of the toothbrush).  (3) The charger is also bigger because the HealthyWhite came with a separate travel charger that was really compact.  (4) The travel case for the Essence 5600 is a soft case, which is unlike the HealthyWhite's hard case.  Although the Essence 5600's case is smaller, the hard case for the HealthyWhite allows you to carry two brush heads.

I've used Sonicare for years after my dentist pointed out that I had really bad plaque.  The improvement in using this toothbrush has been incredible - no more endless poking and scraping at the 6 month check up anymore.The Essence line is the cheapest of the Sonicare line featuring only a 2 minute timer.  I'm a little peeved that they've stripped the 30 second interval timer from the cheaper models (they do throw in a travel bag...which I tossed...).  The replacement heads aren't as sturdy as the old ones either - the "flex" piece and narrow neck are more prone to breakage. But I'm loyal to this brand since it's worked well for me.This also comes with a charger and the standard size head...and I use the compact, so I had to purchase that separately.

My older Sonicare stopped working & would not hold a charge. Since I had recently purchased new brush heads for my old one I wanted a replacement that was compatible.  This one arrived promptly & does as described.

I am a dental hygienist of 25+ years. The Phillips sonicare is the toothbrush that we recommend for our patients who just don't master manual toothbrushes. It is the BEST one for removing plaque which is your enemy and the cause of all forms of dental disease! I have read about this model not having the 30 second timer to time your brushing per arch, but COME ON people. We are all grown up enough to count to 30 and switch arches. For this price the brush is phenomenal! And yes they do take some getting used to, and don't ever start the brush unless it is IN your mouth, unless you enjoy cleaning your bathroom, especially the mirror! The heads are expensive to buy, but it is far cheaper than being referred to the Periodontists officeI have been using my Sonicare toothbrush for several years. It took only a short time to get used to the feel of the toothbrush's vibration. After brushing, my teeth feel much cleaner than they did with a manual toothbrush.Despite being three years old, the Sonicare toothbrush still holds a normal charge, which usually lasts about a week.My only complaint is the cost of the replacement heads, ~$36 for four. If you change them as often as Sonicare recommends, you could spend a small fortune for the replacement heads. Actually the heads last considerably longer than what is recommended by Sonicare. I usually rely more on the appearance of the brush head than the color indicator that supposedly show when a brush head should be replaced. When I start seeing the bristles fanning out, I replace the head.It is an excellent product and I would strongly recommend it to others, if they can afford the ongoing cost of the replacement heads.

I've never really thought about giving an electric toothbrush a try...Like an electric razor, it seemed pointless: Why get an expensive gadget, when I can do the exact same thing on my own, for a fraction of the price? I went in with an open mind, and I immediately disliked the results.....I felt like my teeth and head were going to vibrate apart after twenty or thirty seconds. I diligently kept using it, three times a day, and after a few days, I got used to the vibrating sensation, and started to appreciate how clean my teeth felt after brushing. The toothbrush holds a charge for a long time, and I was impressed at the ingenious design of the charging station...the toothbrush just sits atop the charger, and charges without having to be positioned just so, or making sure Part A is nestled firmly against Part B. It comes with a little carrying case that appears to be able to hold the toothbrush and the charging station (I haven't actually tried it to see if they all fit.....), and the toothbrush comes apart easily for quick cleaning. That said, I was less than thrilled to find that the various parts of the toothbrush are NOT watertight, and will admit water, toothpaste, and saliva. That kind of grossed me out, so now I take it apart and clean it and dry it every day. Also, you'll want to start brushing topless and not looking it the mirror, because the spit will be flying, and will make quite a mess. Keep your mouth closed, and brush bent over the sink!Overall, not a bad product. It remains to be seen if I'm fully won over by the electric toothbrush, but, for now, I'm digging this one.
I like this toothbrush very much but would be nice to have the 30 second timer.  I had one of these several years ago and tried another brand.  This is definitely the best.

This rechargeable toothbrush surprised me in a couple of ways. First, how quiet it was compared to other electric toothbrushes I have used in the past. And secondly, how far it threw the toothpaste when I turned it on. The first time I used it, I simply put a dollop of toothpaste on the bristles and turned it on. BIG MISTAKE! The brush threw toothpaste right off into the sink and sent tiny white spots all over my freshly painted bathroom wall. After cleaning up the tiny dots (smaller than a pencil lead) I tried again, this time I squeezed the toothpaste INTO the bristles and put it my mouth before turning it on. Worked much better this way.Performance was excellent, doing a very good job of cleaning my teeth with out causing any discomfort at all. I have to say that my teeth feel much cleaner after using the Sonicare Electric Toothbrush than when using a manual toothbrush. Worth the money.

I really like this toothbrush. It's tough compactness ensures a good grip and the simplicity of its "control interface" (only one button!) is also a pleasing experience.The problems: I have no idea how much battery life is left--I do appreciate the other Sonicare brushes with the battery life LED levels. The other Sonicare brush I've used also had a pretty good feature--it'd buzz when it was time to move from one surface side to another. The total brushing time for this beast is supposed to be two minutes so my silly yearning for scientific toothbrushing (I kid, but not really!) is spoiled because I have to guess when to move from the top outside surfaces to the inside.I actually like this, but some may not: there's a bit of ferocity in there. According to the manual, the brush allows 12 uses before it switches to full strength, but I didn't notice that. It was an assault from the get-go. Again, I like this and it makes me feel like it's doing a great job.Battery life is very good in spite of the lack of battery level marker. I've been using the brush everyday for almost a month without noticing a drop in brushing buzz strength. I also like the carrying case which is small and holds the brush head and body quite well.Four stars.

Sonicare likes to keep this style around to offer more affordable entry into the Sonicare toothbrush line.  It is a bit surprising that this larger model with the older style brush head connection is still around and being sold as new.  Compared to the older version this model does offer a travel pack and better grip, but doesn't seem to offer much else new.  Regardless of that, Sonicare is still my favorite when it comes to electric toothbrushes and this version is a good value even with the older design.  For those not wanting to spend a lot on a brush this version offers long batter life, two minute timer, charging indicator, and travel pouch.  If you are new to Sonicare just remember one thing, the replacement heads are not cheap compared to the actual cost of the whole Sonicare brush itself. Obviously Phillips money making is in the replaceable heads and not in the actual selling of the brush.The one thing I do not like about this style of brush is the head connection itsef.  Water and toothpaste can get into the head connection and it gets kind of nasty looking after a while. My wife uses this one style and she has to flush out the head connection with hot water to get all of that out, or replace the head itself.  I have the thinner higher priced whitening model and it does not have this design so I do not have to wash out the head connection.One thing to keep in mind when you are considering one is the cost of this model compared to the thinner, lighter, and better designed higher priced models.  If you travel a lot having the thinner model might be the way to go as the batter still lasts over 7 days and it is easier and lighter to carry.
So far I have been very satisfied with this toothbrush.  Those not accustomed to electric brushes might be surprised by the intensity of the vibration, however most people adapt quickly.
For the price it works great. It runs for 120 seconds and you have to think when 30 seconds has passed to move to the next spot.
My husband's, my daughter's and son's and my Sonicare all stopped working simultaneously after probably 10 years. I bought 4 of these as replacements, and like it almost as much as the original.

This is my second sonic toothbrush and I felt the need to replace the old one when it cease recharging. It does not, however, replace manual|mechanical brushing and I would not recommend using it alone as the only toothbrush.

I've been a Sonicare user for a number of years based on the recommendation of my dentist. I've had to have quite a lot of reconstructive dental work and my dentist wise surmised that I want it to keep in its current condition as long as possible and he recommended Sonicare as stacking the deck in my favor. I'm sold on Sonicare in general.Should you get a Sonicare? Yes. Without a doubt.This particular unit, the HX5610/01, is actually my 2nd Sonicare. It's what I will call the 'basic' model and I consider my 'weekend house' toothbrush.  I also have thePhilips Sonicare HX6732/02 HealthyWhite R732 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrush, a 'deluxe' version, that I consider my day-to-day Sonicare. Both units are the crème of the crop tools when it comes to cleaning teeth. What sets the 'basic' model apart from my 'deluxe' Sonicare is seemingly minor, but is worth mentioning. To appreciate the difference, you have to know how you use the brush.To properly use Sonicare, you use it for 2 minutes. Exactly 2 minutes. 30 seconds on the top teeth on the outside, 30 second on the top teeth on the inside of your mouth, 30 seconds on the bottom teeth on the outside, and finally 30 seconds on the bottom teeth on the inside of your mouth.The 'deluxe' model has a small 'beep' every 30 seconds to keep you on track. The 'basic' model has no beep, no cues to keep you moving along. It seems minor, and in the scheme of life, it is. It's just that I really find my self *wanting* that feature on the 'basic' model. It's just not there. This seems like a fairly arbitrary omission on Philips' part. This omission is the only reason I have given this brush 4 stars instead of 5.There are some cosmetic differences between the 2 units. The 'basic' unit is heavier than the 'deluxe' model. Lastly, you can't share brush heads between these 2 units because they have different connectors at the base of the brush heads.I prefer the more expensive model, but for double the price, I'm not sure its 'must-have' feature I find so valuable in the more expensive model should sway you to the more expensive version. You may not miss it at all. Especially if I hadn't told you about it. Sorry about that.

My hygienist recommended getting a Sonicare, but they are much more costly than manual toothbrushes and I wasn't sure I would like them.So, I bought a cheap, battery operated electric toothbrush at a local store.  I liked the feel of it, but after only a couple of months, it wouldn't turn on anymore.  I thought it was just dead batteries at first, so I replaced them.  Nothing.  Another set of fresh batteries.  Nothing.So I bought this lower-end Sonicare.  I don't really need all the bells and whistles of the more expensive ones.  And I LOVE the way this one works.  It took some getting used to, and I hit my teeth with the hard part of the brush when I first tried, but it didn't take long to adapt.  Also, it has a lower setting for the first several times of use, allowing someone new to this type of brush to get used to it.  My teeth and mouth feel so clean after I use it.It is supposed to beep twice when it is in the lower speed mode, and once in the higher.  Mine, after fully charged according to the light on the charger, didn't beep at all.  I called customer service, and they said I needed to put the brush in the charger to see if it beeped.  That worked, but since then, I can't get it to beep at all.  Not a big deal, but still, not working quite as advertised.I chose this rechargeable one instead of the battery operated lower-end model because, with my cheap electric toothbrush I got first, I didn't like the feel when the batteries start losing power.  Instead of just stopping, the brush just slowed and got less effective.  I assumed that this would do the same but I don't know that for a fact.I do wish the replacement heads were not so expensive, but I found that they are pretty expensive for any of the better electric toothbrushes, so this one is not out of line with those.  I'm willing to spend the extra money for the improvement in brushing.

I initially gave this Sonicare toothbrush 3 stars because my experience with it was rather ticklish, plus I found that "brushing" with it is kind of a hassle.  With a typical non-motorized toothbrush I'm fond of wandering about when I brush.  That's not recommended with this gizmo, since I've found it impossible to "brush" without foam dribbling down my chinSince then, I found that the ticklish sensation has gone away -- this was more attributable to the "learning phase" than anything else.  Once the brush reached full speed it stopped feeling annoying.I have noticed that the Sonicare brush has kept tea stains down significantly -- which is why I purchased it -- so I'm quite happy about that.  I still don't love the experience, but I do like it, especially the results!  4 stars!
Great.
I love the Philips Sonicare products and this one did not disappoint.  A great way to get the kids to brush 2 minutes at time. Very fast delivery and great product.

I've been using Sonicare electric toothbrushes for many years and have nothing but positive things to say about them; the Phillips Sonicare Essence 5600 is no different.This is the fourth Sonicare brush I've had.  The other 3 are the FlexCare, Healthy White, and the older Elite models.  The Essence 5600 is similar in design to the Elite series brushes.  It has a larger body and a screw on type brush head.  The major advantage this model has over all the other models I've owned is that it has stronger action in the brush head.  My teeth feel like they get a more thorough cleaning when using this brush.A cool feature for anyone new to using an electric toothbrush is the Ramp-up mode. This allows your gums to slowly get adjusted to the action of the brush head over the first twelve uses.  My first electric toothbrush didn't have this feature and it took me awhile to get used to it.Another plus is that it's convenient for traveling.  The Essence 5600 lasts up to two weeks on a single charge and also comes with a cloth carrying pouch.The biggest disadvantage for me is the design of the brush head.  Fluids are more prone to collect in the bottom part of the head between the body and where the head screws on.  My FlexCare and Healthy White brushes attach to the body right at the base of the neck so fluid doesn't collect on the inside.  With the Essence 5600,  I have to clean out fluids twice as often.Despite this issue,  I'd still recommend this product.  The Sonicare Essence 5600 is a solid performer.

This is such a fantastic toothbrush, I love using it.  My teeth feel so clean after using it, I actually brush more than before.  The 2 minute timer is really helpful, as I can't say that I would usually brush that long using my manual toothbrush. My dentist has been recommending these for years, telling me how good they are for my gums and for keeping plaque at bay. We'll see what she says at my next cleaning.The toothbrush is nice to hold, not loud and holds a charge for quite a long time.  I pack it with me using the travel case as I brush my teeth after lunch, but I'm not buying another one for the office - yet.  Coming from a manual toothbrush to this makes me wonder, what took me so long?It is quite no frills and there are models that do more, but at the heart of the Sonicare is an excellent toothbrush. I like this so much, I might put this one at the office and get a different model for home, maybe with the quad pacer and different modes. I like it very much and I am rating it four stars and will reserve the fifth after my dentist visit.

The sonicare Essence is a good toothbrush especially if you are short on cash.  It does let you know when the 2 minute brush time is up by shutting off.  It worked well but  it did not have the quadpacer feature to tell me when to switch to another part of my mouth. My old sonicare the Advance had that feature and it kept by brushing time more on point. I was lost when I started brushing with the Essence.  Without the quadpacer I had to guess at how much time to spend on each part of my mouth.  I often went through the two minute brush time twice trying to make sure I got every part of my mouth clean.  The brush heads wore out kinda fast too. I don't think my brush heads made it to the 3 month mark. In the end I would give it a good rating.  I believe in the Sonicare brand.  My dentist recommended it to me and I have never been disappointed.  I have sensitive teeth and this toothbrush was gentle to my teeth and gums.  This brush kept my teeth and gums pretty healthy. My mouth would always feel clean and fresh after brushing. I would recommend this toothbrush but I wish they would add the  timing feature to it. It's a good toothbrush to try if you are new to Sonicare or electric toothbrushes.

This sonic toothbrush is fine. I had an Oral B sonic toothbrush before this one and loved it but it died and Oral B no longer makes it. This one was a great price and the functionality and feel of it is fine but not great. Comparing it to my previous toothbrush this is what I don't like - this has no 30 second or 2 minute alert, the fact that it doesn't have a 30 second alert doesn't really bother me but it just stops at 2 minutes. My previous toothbrush alerted you at 2 minutes but didn't stop. It is just so abrupt when it stops, I don't like that. This toothbrush also seems to have a much stronger vibration or maybe it's the brush head itself. It doesn't feel as nice - sorry I can't describe it better. I think will try the sensitive brush head when this one wears out and see if that one feels better as some other reviewers have mentioned.The other thing I don't like, and I knew it when I bought it, is the cost of the replacement heads. They are expensive and seem to be overkill. I wish you could just replace the top part instead of the whole big thing.Bottom line - it's a good toothbrush and for the price it's very good. I don't regret buying it I'm just not delighted with it.

As far as cleaning your teeth, you just can't beat a Sonicare. I've had Braun and Oral-B toothbrushes in the past and they don't come close to the Sonicare in the way they clean my teeth.  You know that feeling after you come out of the dentist's office for a cleaning where your teeth have that polished, smooth feeling when you run your tongue over them?  Even after the first time using this one my teeth felt that way.  Another nice thing was that my gums don't bleed when I use this toothbrush. For as long as I can remember my gums have bled after brushing my teeth, even when brushing by hand.  I thought with the way this brush moves so fast that it would cause bleeding too but for some reason it doesn't.Compared to myPhilips Sonicare HX6711/02 HealthyWhite 710 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushthis one has a bigger brush head. Instead of replacing just the brush on the stemPhilips Sonicare HX6013/90 ProResults Brush Head Standard, 3-Packyou have to replace the whole top partPhilips Sonicare HX7002/30 e-Series Replacement Brush Heads, 2-Pack (Frustration Free Packaging)which is more difficult to unscrew and replace.  It also is more difficult to clean because it doesn't just pull out like my old one, and it's possible to get fluids under this brush base so you do have to clean it. The whole toothbrush isn't as compact as my older model either.  The charging base is larger and the handle of the brush is wider.  What I do like better about this model is that the movement is a little stronger than my older model, the brush seems to move faster and have a little more power.  I think it cleans my teeth faster. (Not that 2 minutes with either toothbrush is all that big of a deal.)The brush has a two minute timer like my older model.  It stops after 2 minutes and you have to restart it again if you want to continue. (Why does 2 minutes seem so long when you're in a hurry?)  My older model had little pauses that you could hear after each 30 seconds so you knew how long you were spending on each part of your mouth (Quadpacer Technology). This model doesn't have that, but I didn't really pay that much attention to it anyway. Both are easy to take along when you travel.  This one has a nice little carrying case.  Once it's charged it will last for up to 2 weeks without being in the charger base. I don't know how long the older one would hold a charge because it was compact enough to take the whole thing along when we traveled so I never let the charge run down.So I guess my rating would actually be a 4-1/2.  I like that it seems a little more powerful than myPhilips Sonicare HX6711/02 HealthyWhite 710 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushbut I don't like that the brush assembly is more expensive to replace and more difficult to disassemble and clean. Does it get my teeth whiter than the old model?  Well, my teeth are probably as white as they are going to get because I think that all Philips Sonicare brushes do an excellent job so I don't see a difference between the two models in how they clean.  It's all just a matter of what features are important to you.

Pros:-Sonicare quality product-One of the best electric toothbrushes on the marketCons:-Gen 2 Brush head (will grow mold if not taken off and dried correctly each time you brush)-carrying case instead of extra brush head-NI-MH battery-Expensive for what it is now-No 30 second timer beep (don't really need it, but some people are spoiled, lol)For the price I paid, I could have gotten cheaper on Black Friday with an extra brush head too. Oh well, the carrying case is alright. After about 2 weeks use, my teeth are healthier, whiter, and smoother. Sonicare brushes do work, if you don't have one, or know a family member that doesn't. Do yourself a favor and spend a little to save you teeth for the future, unless you want dentures.

it's a really nice toothbrush, the junctions (base/handle, handle/head) are all electromagnetic so you don't have to worry about rusty or shoddy contacts. the thing chirps at 2 minutes and then turns off to warn you that you should get carried away with your oral hygiene. comes with a nice case so you can brush your teeth while you're driving in traffic.saw this at target for $30. don't buy this like it's a blue light special, because it's not, but it is a very nice affordable electric toothbrush, and the buzzing feels like you're chewing on a big old vibrator.

I like the way my teeth feel after I use this toothbrush. I also love that it goes for 2 minutes then shuts itself off so you know you're cleaning for the correct amount of time. Be careful however on not hitting your other teeth with it. It will vibrate the hell out of you. It's worth a buy but the replacement heads are costly. This does NOT come with an extra toothbrush head. Just the one.

I love the way my teeth and gums feel super clean after using it.It is comfortable to hold and use. The charge lasts for at least a week. My two complains are: the charger barely holds the brush in place, it is very easy for it to fall down, so I have to put it on a flat surface to charge; also, the brush was very hard on my gums for first few days, but now I have gotten used to it.

This was a replacement for same model that he's had for about 6 years.  He loves it, and obviously it's had a good life span.  I replaced mine (after about 5 years) with the Oral B Pulsonic toothbrush which I prefer because it's much smaller to hold, and travel with (holds about a 2 week charge), and brush replacements less expensive.  But for the big guy (or gal), the Sonicare is great.
better than having to buy two handles for my son who only needed one toothbrush. he loves the green handle on the grip.

I bought this to replace a Sonicare Elite because it's battery was weakening to an extreme. This new toothbrush seems to work as well as the now departed toothbrush though it comes with a few less perks. There are no 30 second beeps with this basic Sonicare to alert the user to move to a new quadrant. There's just the one beep at the end.Compared to the previous toothbrush, this one is heavier with a shorter and thicker handle. I do like that this Essence has one flat side to keep it from rolling off the sink counter.Another good point is that the unused brushes from the Elite fit this toothbrush also so they won't go to waste.Bottom line -  Those who are used to using a Sonicare and getting good reports from their hygienists should be satisfied with this basic Sonicare. I don't think it's worth paying over twice as much to get those added timer beeps.
I enjoy the clean feeling of my teeth after I use it and hope it helps keep plaque from causing detrimental effects on my gums.

As a longtime Sonicare user (more than 15 years now) I've used multiple iterations of their electric toothbrushes and they just keep getting better and better. This model, designed to be an entry level addition to their collection, doesn't have all the bells and whistles of thePhilips Sonicare HX6972/10 FlexCare Plus Rechargeable Electric Toothbrush, but it does have everything you need. In fact, we own a FlexCare, and in a few days of trying the Essence, I can't say we really missed to much between the two. (How often do you even really use the extra settings and programs? For me at least, I know it's not that often.)The travel case is handy and the charging stand incredibly compact (and will work with other Sonicare products).The previous Essence model can be found for as little as $40 now (Philips Sonicare HX5351/30 Essence 5300 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrush) and that price for a Sonicare is a real bargain.Simple and effective, once you try a Sonicare, you'll never go back to regular brushing. It's made a huge difference at our bi-yearly dental checks and my son, now 5, has been using aPhilips Sonicare HX6311/02 Sonicare for Kids Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushsince he started brushing (it's holding up nicely, by the way) and (knock wood) has never had a single cavity. (You'd be shocked and appalled at the number of kids in his class that have multiple cavities or even pulled teeth due to bad or no brushing <shudder>.)In short, I've tried other brands, but Sonicare, to me, remains the gold standard of getting that "dentist smooth and clean" feel at home.
The sound is loud, I wish that the rechargeable is universal so it can be carried on travelling to other countries where have different output.

This replaces a previous sonicare that wore out, This one shuts off after the recommended 2 minutes. Now if I would just use it more often. It works great. I have to take the brush off every other week and clean out the well where toothpaste has dripped down the shaft & it is a little hard to get inside with fat fingers but a damp handkerchief works great.

This is second Sonicare toothbrush we purchased - first one, set of 2 purchased at Costco, lasted 4 years with daily use.  Company has excellent customer service (replaced defective toothbrush purchased @ Costco quickly with no questions.

I used to own the Oral-b Sonic and had used it for the past three years.  I loved it.  It worked great until my son knocked it off the counter.  Anyway, I was very happy to have a chance to try out the Pro Care 1000.  It actually seems to be a bit stronger than my old Sonic.  Of course, like I said, I owned it for awhile so I don't know if they get weaker in time.  The price of the Pro Care is quite a bit less than the Sonic.  Therefore, I think the Pro Care is a much better deal.  The base unit is also smaller than that of the Sonic.  The Sonic didn't take up THAT much space, anyway, but still...So, in my opinion, the Oral-b toothbrushes are excellent.  And, I would go with the Pro Care over the Sonic because of price and power.Are they helpful with cleaning?  Yes.  I actually haven't had any cavities for the past 3 years and I think a lot of that success is due to using my Oral-b.  Get one.  :)

The Oral-B Professional Healthy Clean Precision 1000 is a nice electric toothbrush.  Unlike some cheaper brands, this unit oscillates as opposed to simply vibrating.  It is not super cheap but nor is it overly expensive.  On a high level, people wonder why use a powered toothbrush instead of a manual toothbrush.  It is arguable whether the powered brushes clean any better, but if you have limited mobility, are arthritic, etc. a powered brush can be very helpful.What's in the box?* Oral-B Professional Healthy Clean precision 1000 rechargeable toothbrush.* One toothbrush head.* Rechargeable handle.* Charging base.* Covered toothbrush head case.* Instruction booklet.SETUP:Preparing this brush for use is simple.  Plug in the charger, place the toothbrush on the base to begin charging.  Later, snap on the toothbrush head - that's it.The included brush head case attaches directly to the charging base if desired.  It may be left separate if you like.  The case is covered and holds up to four brush heads.  This is convenient if you use the brush for several people in a family, etc.The unit operates on a NiMH battery.  The battery is not replaceable by the consumer, which is a disadvantage.FUNCTIONALITY:There is only one button on the until, the power button.  The brush has one speed which is called Daily Use.  Some higher priced Oral-B models have three modes:  Daily Use, Sensitive, and Whitening.
