The Daily Clean mode does a good job of brushing, and my teeth feel "clean" after use.The battery takes a good deal of time for the initial charge, advertised as 17 hours.  The brush may be left on the charger so that it is "always charged".  The unit CANNOT be used in a corded mode, so it operates only in a cordless fashion.  The brush holds a charge for a good period, so that you will get a couple of weeks of brushing, at 2 minutes each 2 times a day.The unit has two display icons: One that is shaped like a battery which lights green and blinks while the battery is charging, and the other icon is shaped like an electrical plug and lights amber when the unit needs to be charged.The unit includes an alerting system, which it kind of pauses every 30 seconds to alert you to move on to the "next quadrant" of teeth.  It pauses again at the end of 2 minutes (the assumed average time to brush your teeth.)  I think the 30 second interval is overkill, especially since tooth-brushing is a personal habit, and people brush different amounts of time on different areas of their mouth, some people may have more or fewer teeth, etc.Cleanup is pretty simple.  You remove the brush head and rinse it as you would any other toothbrush.  The handle and charger should not get wet!Overall, the brush performs well and works as advertised.  It has a good price point and a nice 2 year warranty.  It is simple to use, carry with you, and keep clean.  It also has several different brush heads available for purchase.  So if you are in the market for a powered toothbrush, give this one a look.PS - I think Oral-B does need a better (shorter) product name!  "Oral-B Professional Healthy Clean Precision 1000" is a bit long.

This is the most basic of rechargeable electric toothbrushes, but it works very well. The charge lasts for quite a while, and while it doesn't feature automatic shut-off, it does beep every 30 seconds to make it easier for users to know when the 2-minute recommended brushing period has elapsed. The handle is very reasonably sized (in fact, my 5-year-old daughter has been the recipient of this toothbrush) for people of all ages and sizes. It does come with a charging stand add-on for more brush-heads if you happen to be sharing with other family members. All-in-all, it's a great basic brush that would work for any member of your family!

I got this toothbrush hoping to replace my old Sonicare Advance a4100.  It's smaller on the counter, doesn't have an opening under the brush head base to trap water and mold (I have to take my Sonicare top off and dry out the hole and the base of the brush head every darn time I use it to keep this crud from growing there) and has a wide range of inexpensive brush choices.  But, so far I haven't found it to do as good a job of cleaning my teeth, at least as far as I can tell based on the way my mouth feels after brushing.  My mouth and teeth feel noticeably cleaner and fresher after using the Sonicare versus using this Oral-B product.  So, I'm going to continue to try this one from time to time, but I think I'll stick with the Sonicare until it dies.  It's also worth noting that this Oral-B brush is quite a bit less expensive than the Sonicare and replacement brushes are a fraction of the cost of the ones for my Sonicare.  So, this product has a number of things going for it, but it just doesn't perform quite up to the level of its higher cost competitor in my unscientific testing.

I have been using a water pick, floss, and regular toothbrush, but thought I would give this electric toothbrush a try to add to my repertoire of tooth and gum care.At first I loved the toothbrush and my teeth felt so clean afterwards (that feeling after the dental hygienist polishes your teeth), but after a bit of use I really missed my ol' standby manual toothbrush. I didn't feel like the electric brush got under the gum line and I was having a bit of pain on the gums since the electric brush is pretty rough and fast rotation speed.Also, I had a bit of trouble holding the electric brush, for some reason every once in a while it would go flying out of my hand (with great amusement and laughter).I really like this new gadget and will continue to use for "polishing" my teeth, but will continue to use my regular manual brush for gum health.

I was intrigued by this, as I've always wanted to try one of those toothbrushes you see at the dentist's office. Thanks to Vine, I got a chance. I've been using this since I got it and it's wonderful but I do have a few misgivings.First the good. This is a nice little toothbrush and it gives a really good clean. My teeth feel nice and smooth after using it and my gums feel pretty great as well. As compared to non-electric toothbrushes, this will give a superior clean. I also like that the head is smaller, which makes it easier to fit in my mouth at various angles. My mouth is a little on the small side, so huge toothbrush heads tend to be a little awkward. There's also a little container for you to keep various heads in, so if you want the entire family to use this (and at eighty dollars, why not) then you have storage. These are all things I'd praise about this brush.However, there are a few quibbles. Most of them are minor and didn't entirely take away from the product itself, so I didn't feel right detracting an extra star for them, as they wouldn't bother everyone. The only thing I took a star off for was for the first reason. First off, my brush would occasionally stop and start on rare occasions. I'm not sure if this was just a random glitch or if I didn't put the head on firmly enough, but it was a little annoying. (I pushed it down pretty securely though.) Secondly, I wasn't happy that this only came with one head and it only came in one speed. I'm aware that these brushes do get fancier and that usually means more money, but the price tag on this isn't cheap and you can purchase a basic brush that goes somewhat fast for far less. This cleans incredibly well and looks to be sturdier than some of the electric brushes I've had in the past, but if you're on a budget I'm not sure that there's enough here to really justify a toothbrush of this price range. I also was a little disappointed that the battery was non-replaceable. Once it stops re-charging (which admittedly isn't a short life span) you can't replace the battery. You should get years of use out of this, but that just sort of bugs me. Not a biggie, though.Overall this is a pretty spiffy brush. I absolutely love that the head is smaller and far easier to fit in my mouth. I love that it gives a really great clean and leaves my teeth feeling nice and smooth. (As close as you'll get to that "just from the dentist feeling" without going to the dentist or buying a three hundred dollar toothbrush.) I'm just disappointed at the lack of options for a toothbrush at this price range and at how the brush would have a brief little moment where it stopped for a fraction of a second. If you want more options but don't want to spend a lot, I'd recommend one of the water brushes out there. I've previously tried out theInterplak Sonic Water Jetand really loved it, especially the water floss. It's about half the price and has several speeds of water. I am aware that not everyone likes water brushes, though, so if you have the cash and want a nice electric brush then this one is worth checking out.

This is a nice electric toothbrush that does a good job of cleaning your teeth. I was disappointed that it was missing the brushes when I received it, but I was able to purchase Oral-B replacement brushes, although they cost more than I thought they would! I liked that they included colored rings to fit on the brushes to allow more than one family member to use the unit and keep track of which brush is theirs.Once I had the brushes, it took over 12 hours for the unit to charge initially. After that, it has worked fine. It's easy to use with clear direction on use of the brush.I like the convenient storage place for the brushes that is attached to the base. Those with sensitive teeth or gums may prefer theOral-B Professional Healthy Clean + Sensitive Gum Care Precision 3000 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushthat has a lower setting. However, this model is still a good toothbrush that is easy to use and works well.

We have used these for AGES,,,they have recently updated the battery and charging system, and needed to be replaced. These can also take the small round heads, or the larger more regular toothbrush looking heads - both work great, and the toothbrush has a time that lets you know when you have hit every quadrant of your mouth successfully and lets you know when your have finished up! Nice gadget! *On a side bonus...you can clean your rings with it!

I have tried the Oral-B electric toothbrush before, and I came away still loving my Sonicare.  I tried it again, wondering if perhaps I was too harsh in my review of the Oral-B, but I still love my Sonicare.  There are some aspects of the Oral-B that I do like, however there are a few points that say "no way" to me.  For instance, the thickness of the brush head is a big deal killer for me.  I measured the brush head of both my Sonicare and the Oral-B.  The Sonicare brush (I like the compact size) measurement from the middle of the brush head is 10mm (1 cm), and the Oral-B is 18mm.  That is almost twice as thick as the Sonicare.  When I am brushing and going around from the side to the front, the brush pops out of my mouth and lips because I have a smaller mouth.  If you have a big mouth with lots of teeth, maybe this is not an issue for you.  Thanks to my orthodontist in the 70s I had four plus my four wisdom teeth removed, so I feel like I have hardly any teeth, the arch of my teeth is a lot smaller than someone with a full set of teeth.I do like the way the Oral-B brushes behind my front teeth.  This is why I prefer the compact size Sonicare brush.  I don't like the way the Oral-B buzzes in between quadrants, and I really don't like the way it makes a weird buzzing, stop-start thing when your two minutes are up, and then continues to go.  The Sonicare just stops after two minutes.  If you want to continue, just press the button again.My final assessment of the Oral-B is personal preference.  I still prefer my Sonicare.

When I first ordered this toothbrush I figured I would be writing a negative review. but this electric toothbrush has won me over with its ease of use and cleaning power.  In particular, it does a fantastic job of getting in between teeth, or at least shoving toothpaste in between your teeth.  The smaller head allows you to get in back of and around your chompers easier.I knocked off a star because this model claims to have a sensor that lights up if you press too hard against your teeth.  I tried (to the point of nearly snapping the brush head off) but could never get the sensor to work.  It also stops and starts every 30 seconds.  This is supposed to be a notification to switch to brushing a different "quadrant" in your mouth but I find it just annoys me and disrupts my brushing.  Have we become so dumb that we need a reminder on our toothbrush?Aside from these minor gripes, I really do believe I am getting a superior brushing of the teeth and gums compared to a manual toothbrush and even one of those throwaway "spinner" brushes.  Twenty bucks for three refill heads isn't a horrible price.  You'll pay more in the long run if you switch to this versus sticking to cheap manual toothbrushes.  Perhaps you'll get your money back in the form of smaller dental bills.
I bought htis for my mom and she is really impressed with this and she like the way it works and she feels very comfortble with this tooth brush, thank you Oral-B

I used to have really bad breath in the morning although I brushed my teeth with a regular toothbrush the night before - so my dentist recommended I get an oscillating. I hate the way it feels in my mouth, I guess because I'm not used to it but now when I brush my teeth at night it doesn't smell in morning. More than I could ask for.

I have had great check-ups since going to electric toothbrushes. They do a thorough job and leave your mouth feeling clean. This particular model runs a bit slower in revolutions than the higher end models, but I simply did not need the extra features and expense of the more expensive ones. It takes different Oral B brush heads (all expensive). Takes all night and half a day to charge fully. I rinse the brush as recommended and the handle then dry, after every use, but wished there was a brush cover.

Though not quite in the "league" of SoniCare, this device is most certainly of considerably greater quality and effectiveness than the "garden variety" battery-powered supermarket types. It certainly has had appeal for a health-conscious adolescent who was otherwise indifferent to electic toothbrushing units.

Having never owned anything other than a manual toothbrush before, I was ecstatic to be a guinea pig for Oral-B'sProfessional Healthy Clean Precision 1000 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrush, as I'd always wanted an electric toothbrush. The whole contraption is pretty easy to use and figure out, but I read through the instructions anyway, which included lots of helpful information. When charging on the small base, which can either be hooked to the brush head holder (that holds four heads) or left by itself, it flashes green until fully charged and then the light goes off. When it needs charged, another light will flash red. When in use, every thirty seconds the toothbrush will vibrate or stutter to remind the user to move on to a different quadrant (they break it up into fourths, illustrations in instructional pamphlet) during the recommended two minute brushing. My teeth feel buffed and my whole mouth feels much cleaner using this, and it obviously does a much better job than a manual, so I'm more than happy. I've noticed my teeth are even whiter and this is without a whitening mode. I like the fact that there are many interchangeable brush heads to suit different purposes or preferences too. I've seen some reviewers mention the fact that they can't get the other brushing modes to work, that is because there are no other modes. This is the 1000 model, so there's only the one. If interested in having more, theOral-B Professional Healthy Clean + Sensitive Gum Care Precision 3000 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushhas the three modes mentioned in the manual. The product page for some of the other Oral-B Professional Healthy Clean Precision models on Amazon has a nice table (and also videos) that shows all the different models to pick the right one for your needs (Oral-B Professional Care 1000 Electric Toothbrush, 1 Count), which would be handy and help with the confusion if it was added onto the 1000's product details. I would definitely recommend this as a step up from the traditional manual toothbrush and if you're looking for a rather basic electric toothbrush, otherwise you might want to look at a more advanced model.What I like:- base is small so it takes up little space- easy to use: the lights to let me know when to charge or when it's done; the 30-second stuttering telling me to move on now- indicator bristles where the blue will fade eventually so I'll know when to replace the head- the pressure sensor will stop movement of the brush if too much pressure is exerted, I haven't run into that yet- the light blue/white coloring is pleasing, nothing flashy or garish. There's also a darker blue/white that is nice.- instructions are easy to understand, with clear illustrations and tips, the only problem would be it didn't say if this model did have the three brushing modes (daily clean, sensitive, and whitening), I had to figure that out by going online. Again, it only has the one daily clean mode, hence the 1000.- six different brush heads can be used with it (Oral-B Precision Clean Brush Head (4 ct.),Oral B Floss Action 3 pack brush head refill,Oral-B Pro White Replacement Electric Toothbrush Head (1 ct.),Oral-B Sensitive (3 Extra-Soft Brush heads), BRAUN REPLACEMENT ORTHO BRUSH set for use with braces, andOral-B Power Tip Replacement Brush Head, 1 count)Cons:- only one brush head was included, surely they could have added one other- takes some time to charge
Good, trustworthy toothbrush. Interchangeable heads are nice. It's easy to keep clean. Has 30 second timer so you know how long you're brushing is section to get two minutes of brushing in. Like it!

these units are commonplace these days.... what is nice about this unit is, no batteries, and the chargers footprint is barely larger than the brush itself...great for crowded vanity tops...unit works as well as any other spinner I have used..

This is my first electric toothbrush. I wanted to get one because I felt like my manual couldn't get my teeth as clean as I wanted, and my dental hygenist said that I had plaque around my gumline. This toothbrush definitely takes care of that problem. I think it might be slightly too powerful, but I'd rather go a little over the top than not powerful enough.I like how the head is small and you can pinpoint on locations around your mouth, except that I feel that it doesn't hold enough toothpaste. Other reviews have said that they like how you "don't need to use as much toothpaste," but I think you don't use enough. I put toothpaste on it for one side of my mouth, and then stop and reload halfway through for the other half. It's inconvenient, but otherwise I feel like half my mouth gets jipped on the toothpaste and doesn't get as clean.Another con that someone else mentioned--this toothbrush makes it almost impossible to keep from drooling all over. I have to lean over the sink in case I drool and usually stop one once or twice to spit while brushing to prevent it from dripping out of my mouth or ending up with a blue waterfall over my chin.I like how this toothbrush charges. When it's low the light turns red, when it's charging it blinks green, and when it's done charging it goes blank. Easy peasy. I also never felt the toothbrush losing power or become weaker when the red light came on to tell me to charge it.

I've used the Oral-B line of toothbrushes for over ten years, and I have been a happy customer, especially after the move to rechargeable batteries. This particular model is replacing a more moderately pricedOral-B Vitality Precision Clean Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushmodel. The Vitality is much less expensive than this "Professional" model; and it becomes a question of whether one should take the step up.First of all, any Oral-B toothbrush handle has a primary job of giving something to hold onto and drive the movement of the replaceable brush heads. These brush heads are interchangeable; they work whatever handle you buy, and I can't say that the more expensive handle does a better or worse job of driving it. I've been lately sampling theFloss Action Headand I like it quite a bit, especially after eating corn on the cob or pulled pork; and it works just as well on either handle.As for its function as a handle, I actually think the cheaper model is slightly better having a larger diameter, which fits my hand better, and having a slightly rubberized grip, while the Professional is hard plastic.The Professional shines in battery management. There is no easier way to ruin an Oral-B head than to try to use it as a non-electric toothbrush after you've let the battery die out. The Professional has a very convenient warning light that comes on when you are nearing the end of a charge. In my case, this took about a week of 2 a day brushings. So I was able to take it out of the shower and put it on its charger without having to endure a dead or dying battery tomorrow.  For those concerned about such things, the Professional allows you to open up the battery chamber and separately dispose of the battery when it's time to throw it away (which should be many years at least if my experience with the Vitality is a guide).You might want to take time perusing the manual. Even with my years of use, I hadn't been aware that the handle gives a little pulsation every 30 seconds to act as a stopwatch for you to give each section of your mouth a fixed amount of time for a proper cleaning. And man do my teeth feel clean after starting to follow the actual instructions.These Oral-Bs have a very convenient, and safe, inductive charging system which keeps exposed electrical contacts out of your bathroom.In summary, I recommend getting whichever Oral-B toothbrush you can afford. I would say the Professionals battery management is a big plus and might be worth the premium in an upscale household, but you might want to save your money and put it into their floss action heads.

I've used Oral-B rechargeable electric toothbrushes since 1996.  To be honest, I like the early models better than the more recent ones.  My 1996 model lasted trouble-free until 2012, with use at least three times per day.  When my old toothbrush finally went, I took my brand-new spare out of the drawer, one my wife bought around 2006 (thinking my old one would be dying any day), and the switch lasted about two weeks.  (It stayed on, and would not shut off!)  So, I bought this one.  Been using it a couple of weeks and it works fine so far.  I'd give it five stars, but as I said, I don't like the feel of it as much as I did the much earlier model.  (How many times have we heard that said about a product?)  Still, no complaints.  I bought one of these for my wife last year, and it's still going strong--which is why I got this one for myself.  I'd buy another if I needed it.
this brush is good. my dentist said my teeth look better after using it. It works. Pros: small footprint, makes mouth feel clean. Cons: long time to recharge, like 24 - 48 hours.

We like this electric brush.  It is definitely much better than a basic manual brush but it does not irritate our teeth like a Sonic brand.We received a Sonic as a gift but never used it because it was too irritating on our teeth and gums. We then got this Oral-B and it is a really nice electric brush.  Our teeth are cleaner (you can feel the difference) and it is simple and comfortable to use.We really like the timer that helps keep us on track.

My wife always says I brush too hard since my toothbrush heads wear out twice as fast as hers.  When we got this, she thought that I'd finally learn that I was indeed brushing too hard.  Well, either that particular feature is not working on this Oral B toothbrush, or I am brushing with the right pressure.  I did try to really press down hard on it and it never did shut off.  So I'm thinking I might have one where that feature isn't working.  However, reading the other reviews, it sounds like others have gotten it to work.Other than that, this is a really great toothbrush.  My teeth feel especially clean when I go through the whole 2 min routine.  The toothbrush pulsates every 30 seconds to let you know it is time to move to a different quadrant of the mouth.  Without that, I'm sure my brushing would only take me about 80-90 seconds.The charger is small and compact: it won't take much space up on your counter.  I used to have an older model that had a wall mount kit.  This does not have that, but that's no big deal for me.  The booklet says the charge should last a week.  This is a factor for me, since my bathroom doesn't have a convenient outlet for keeping this on the charger every night, so I have the charger in another room and only charge the unit when it needs it.  In actuality, the red "needs a charge" light comes on probably about every 4 days (probably because I put too much pressure on my teeth).Overall, great toothbrush, with the best feature being the timed brushing cycle to ensure a really thorough job.

I'm fortunate to have good teeth, but I don't have a good brushing technique. I use the old back and forth instead of the recommended up and down. It has caused some problems with receding gums from me being too harsh on them. I've been thinking about picking up an electric toothbrush and gave this one a try.I have to say I'm impressed. It recommends that you brush for 2 minutes - 30 seconds in each quadrant of your mouth. To help you achieve that, it pulses every 30 seconds so you know it is time to move on. I have found it has given me better consistency in my brushing and makes me "chill" when I am in a rush and would normally cut things a little shorter. The effect is great, nice smooth teeth that feel like you just left the dentist even though it's been a couple months since my last appointment. I just ordered and received some more heads so that my wife can also use it.The battery seems to last me about a week, which is fine since the charger is handy when it runs low. I'm very happy with it and wish I had started using it long ago.So why only 4 stars? It is very loud. My wife says it sounds like a hedge trimmer, which is a bit of an exaggeration, but not much. If she is asleep when I go to bed, I pull out my manual toothbrush. She wasn't quite as considerate to me this morning when she started brushing with it while I was still sleeping. Even with the bathroom door closed, it woke me up. I'm not sure if the higher end models are quieter, but if they are, it would be worth the extra money.
Only one speed, but it does the job and the price is right.  What more can I say?  Happy New Year.

I have been using these for a while and you really do feel almost like you do after a trip to the dentist after every brush/floss/rinse. Great feeling, good product its a bit pricey but for that feeling it maybe be worth it plus there is a fine rebate right now as well. It seems like good quality item all around-give it a try

PROS: Leaves you with clean feeling mouth and teeth. It has a 30 second timer so that you can brush each quadrant of your mouth for the same amount of time.It turns off when you use too much pressure. This is a nice safety feature but I can't imagine putting that much pressure on your teeth. The brush exerts plenty of pressure on its own.The handle is easier to hold than most other electric toothbrushes. Usually the handle is clunky and awkwardly shaped. Not so with this one.CONS: This toothbrush doesn't have a two minute timer like my last toothbrush (Waterpik). My old brush turns off after two minutes. I tend to zone out while brushing and this little feature is great in letting me know when I'm done. (OK, I admit it, I'm not sticking to the quadrants! After years of brushing all over my mouth at once, I just can't deal with the quadrant routine!)In 6 months, when it's time to let the toothbrush charge down, I won't remember to do it. And even if I do recall it 8-12 months from now, it will be inconvenient. What am I supposed to brush my teeth with while this toothbrush goes on holiday?The manual caused me some confusion by suggesting that there was more than one mode and then not specifying which type of toothbrush had modes and which ones didn't. I'd thrown my box away before looking at the manual and I didn't know if there was more info there or not. This one only has one mode though some other Oral-B toothbrushes must come with daily cleaning, sensitive, and whitening.4 STARS: The head initially feels large if you're used to the regular toothbrush head. For days, I felt like I was using and orbital sander in my mouth. Despite the cons, this toothbrush is worth giving a try. The price is reasonable and it cleans as well as any good electric toothbrush.

My first electric toothbrush and I don't know why I waited so long.This makes brushing fun and easy.  I have a problems with brushing too hard, but with this, I just set the brush on top of each tooth and let it do its job.  My teeth feel clean and slippery afterwards.  Just using the basic brush head alone, my teeth are whiter.  No kidding--I'm a serious tea and coffee drinker.The head itself does a clockwise/counterclockwise rotation.  Turning the head with my hand I get 20 degrees on each side (totaling in 40 degree sweep max).  Its impossible to see how much of that range is utilize with the naked, with the handle turned on.Unit comes with charging station, snap in brush head holder, one brush head and the handle.  The handle has no exposed contacts so I can assume it is an induction type charger.  Basically you don't have to worry about getting it wet when placing the handle on the charger or getting the handle wet when you're cleaning it.  If you choose to not use the snap in holder, it takes up minimal space and it mostly stable.A few cons I found with this toothbrush:- vibrates to the point of tickling my nose- I have to turn the handle off if I take the brush out away from my mouth otherwise, it's toothpaste-saliva everywhere (most noticeably the mirror)- price of replacement heads are steep.  If you're OCD about germs and getting the best brush and are buying for the whole family, then the costs can add up.None of the cons will lower my rating or recommendation.**Update**Vibrations no longer tickle my nose.  I must have become accustomed to it.A feature I forgot to mention above is that there's a 30-second pulse indicator, letting you know 30 seconds has passed.  It does this 3 times which the fourth is the grand finale of pulses and lets you know you've brushed for 2 minutes.  According to the directions, they spilt your teeth into 4 quadrants and the 30 second pulse is to switch quadrants.  I don't always follow it, but I like the feature.

So I think this is a good basic electric toothbrush. The head is tiny so it fits on each tooth and only takes a drop of toothpaste. It vibrates strongly, which I'm still trying to get used to. My skull vibrates and my eyeballs vibrate with it and it's a weird uncomfortable feeling. I found it doesn't reduce the need to floss even a little bit. It seems to clean the surfaces very well. But when I floss afterwards, it's amazing how much stuff still comes out from between the teeth. So it may be beneficial to use an electric toothbrush but if you do a good job on each tooth with a plain old manual toothbrush, I would probably save my money.
Works well. Will stop for a second on occasion when brushing the teeth, but that may be due to adding to much pressure. It does the job and for the price, I would buy one again.

This brush is better than my previous SonicCare electric toothbrush. Sonicare is more expensive and I had to buy 2 in the past year and a half to replace broken ones. This is a basic brush and does a good job. There are a variety of brush heads to suit your needs. I had to get used to the larger brush heads but do not notice anymore. I am pleased with my purchase so far.

I've been using the Philips SonicarePhilips Sonicare HX6932/10 FlexCare RS930 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrushfor a few years now and have to say that the Oral-B Professional Healthy Clean Precision 1000 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrush almost compares to it.Now, there are intrinsic differences that will always separate the two products, the obvious being that the Sonicare vibrates the brush head while the Oral-B oscillates and the difference in brush sizes - the Oral-B brushes a fair deal larger than the Philips which directly translates to comfort preferences for various people.The Oral-B does a fantastic job of cleaning the teeth and gums. The brust tip's gentle oscillating motions never felt too harsh but were strong enough to give me a feeling that the brush was doing it's job of sweeping away plaque build up. The battry life is pretty good from what I've seen.  I've used it for a week without leaving it on the base to charge and the brush is still going strong.A reccommended buy!
I gave these as a gift and my friend thought it was very powerful for a toothbrush, said she liked that

This is my first electric toothbrush and after a few days I can tell that it will get my teeth much cleaner than a regular brush.This model seems to have good battery life, at least initially.  I have used it several times in a week and I have not had to recharge it.  (I'm going to try to drain the battery all the way before recharging, to avoid the "memory" effect).Unfortunately, the stutter feature is really, really annoying.  And AFAIK there is absolutely no way to disable it.  For those who didn't know, after 30 seconds it pauses once for 1 second, after 60 seconds it pauses twice, and after 2 min it pauses 3 times.. or something like that.  I don't brush for a set length of time, I carefully get each side of each tooth.  So I do not need any timer feature, and the stuttering is just really, really annoying.  I wish it could just beep or have an LED light up or something.

The Oral-B Professional Healthy Clean Precision 1000 is a great toothbrush. I've used a Sonicare in the past and the Oral-B leaves my teeth feeling just as clean as the Sonicare at a lower price.The hardest part of the setup is removing the toothbrush from the box (which isn't that hard). Once out you simply attach the brush head and plug in the base.The only draw back I've seen with the Oral-B is that the battery doesn't last near as long as the Sonicare. The Oral-B battery only lasts for 2 or 3 days. The Sonicare battery lasts for more than a week. If you travel a lot you will need to bring the base with you unless you are going on a short trip.Overall, I think the Oral-B Professional Healthy Clean Precision 1000 is an excellent option for anyone looking for an electric toothbrush but doesn't want to pay for a Sonicare.

The first time I used this too brush I had to stop several times because it felt too powerful; however I didn't stop and now my teeth feel much better.  This is a POWERFUL toothbrush that does it's job! I didn't give it five stars yet because I have yet to see my dentist, if they say my teeth have improved I will change the rating to 5 stars.

I usually used the Spinbrush from oral b but it was finally wearing out, so I decided to give this one try based on the reviews. I think it does a decent job. I don't usually have problems with my teeth or gums and dentist has always said my teeth were in very good shape. I have been using this for about a week and it works just as well. The 30 sec timer on it for each area of mouth (4 quadrants 30 sec each) is somewhat noticeable, but not much.I don't go by the 3 month rule. My other brush (oral b spinbrush)  had the same rule about changing heads, but I let mine go for almost 6 months and never had a problem with it wearing down or teeth and gums not getting cleaned, so I am hoping this will last as long. I think the 3 month thing is just more of a way to get people to buy brush heads more often then they need to make more money.  So only buy when you feel it is necessary (teeth and gums not getting as clean as they should) or you really notice the brush heads wearing out.

I have used a Sonicare toothbrush for years and years and years.  In fact, the poor old thing must be more than ten years old.  Given the fact that it probably does not have much more life left in it, I decided to try the Oral-B.Cons:The head is larger than the Sonic, and the first time I used it I found it to be a bit cumbersome.  (Got over that fast, though.)Pros:The Oral-B leaves my teeth feeling extremely clean.It doesn't have a collar (like the Sonicare) that traps gunk beneath it unless frequently cleaned.The unit is made by Braun, which is a very reputable company.The price of the device and the brushes is much more reasonable than the Sonicare.Conclusion:I am going to toss the Sonicare, and stick with the Oral-B.  Time, of course, will tell how durable it is, but for now I am quite satisfied with the product.
I have used Phillips Sonic Care before and I prefer Phillips over Oral-B.  Phillips is also recommended by my dentist.

I have a sonic toothbrush that I really like, but a year ago I found that I needed a second toothbrush to keep at the office and throw in my case for those away from home trips.  I bought this due to name recognition-- I had once had an Oral-B Waterpik I had liked-- and a desire to compare the tooth cleaning experience between the sonic and the plain old electric toothbrush.It came with two heads. One is about the size of a small hand toothbrush which is touted for cleaning and whitening.  The other is a small round one for a "deep and thorough cleaning."  I haven't noticed much difference between the two except the round one is easier to get to my back teeth. Both have indicators show the amount of use.  It is recommended that the brush heads be replaced every three months or when the indicators are half way faded.  Because of the way I use this toothbrush (not all the time) I have not had to replace the heads more than once in the past year.One difference between the sonic toothbrush and this one is that this one stops spinning if too much pressure is applied.The charging station has a small footprint which is very helpful for the old fashioned lavatory I have at the office which has very little space for items kept constantly available.The cleaning experience between the sonic toothbrush and this one is not much different.  Both seem to equally make my teeth and mouth feel fresh.  This toothbrush was quite a bit less expensive than the sonic one.Recommended.

I have used a variety of these for years. This is my second of this type. The first one had a bad battery from the start, and I never got around to replacing it. It would run down sitting in a suitcase on a trip and be quite slow that night in the hotel unless it had charging time first. It never was very fast. Resulted in more stain than usual on my teeth. The new one is better, but not up to the published claims they make about time between charges.However, I believe that this product overall is a critical part of my regimen, and a manual brush or something cheaper would mess with success. I have basically beaten back periodontal disease, such that there is no change for many years now. The other elements of this success include brushing twice daily, flossing once thoroughly at night, and then a thorough water-picking at a low pressure after the floss (with my waterpik purchased from Amazon, of course). It is also important to find a good hygienist who is serious about cleaning out the pockets, and doesn't just give you a quick rush job with a cavitron.

I do love this and prefer it to SonicCare which I had used for over a decade. It's because I like the smaller /  round head. However, the brushes do see to wear faster and why the heck does it have to be so expensive to replace? That aspect really sucks.

This Oral-B Professional Care 1000 Electric Rechargeable Power Toothbrush is  more expensive but definitely works better than my Colgate battery-powered toothbrush. I can see the difference in the little specks of food that get loosened from my teeth. It takes a long time to get fully charged, and seems to lose power a lot sooner than expected, but otherwise, it does provide a good cleaning!

There are no frills here--no extra attachments, no carrying case--just a tiny charger and a simple toothbrush with a single setting. If that setting had a label, it would be: 11.This is the loudest, most powerful toothbrush I've owned. And yes, it leaves you with that "just went to the dentist" clean, smooth feeling if properly used.On the other hand, a sonic brush, with its higher frequency, cleans about as well with less noise. And they're quite cheap these days; theOral-B S-200costs significantly less than this one. Both are sleek, well-designed units. I'm no dental professional, but I don't think it's wise to pay more for a toothbrush just because it has a little more raw power.

I have been using a Philips Sonicare electric toothbrush for several years but wanted to try the Oral B to see if I liked it better - I did not.  The Sonicare vibrates and the Oral B moves more in a circular fashion.I think both do a good job of cleaning the teeth and removing build-up.  But, I happen to like the feel of the Sonicare better.  I think this is just a personal preference and would have no trouble recommending either product.  Both do a significantly better job than a manual toothbrush.The additional items that come with an electric toothbrush are important - if you travel, you should make sure you have a case that will keep your brush clean and will keep the button from being pushed while packed - thus my preference is for a hard plastic case.Battery life on a full charge may be an important consideration for you if you travel for more than a week at a time and do not plan on taking the charger with you.Both companies offer a number of models with different features, which may be of importance or may be useless to you personally - so, before you purchase you should think about how and where you will use your toothbrush and what features will be valuable and which will not.

The stress of a tough year is starting to get to me. I've been puckered up so tight for so long, I'm afraid my puckerer may have frozen that way.So I've been searching for inner peace -- and outer unpuckering -- in personal relaxation products. But expensive gadgets and doodads would just angry my blood up further, so I'm seeking Samadhi on a shoestring budget.Those vibrating chairs and pillows and personal massage doohickeys look pretty inviting -- but they're always so expensive. I figured it's the wiggling that does all of the work, so why not use a vibrating toothbrush to gently caress out my cares? Oral-B, take me away!The only downside is that the head on this brush is rather small, so it takes quite a bit of rubbing on my neck, temples or back to have any real impact. And by the time I've worked out a big knot between my shoulder blades, say, I've created an even bigger one from holding the toothbrush in one place for so long.If this came with some sort of clamp, or maybe a person to hold the brush for you, then it would be a nice massage tool for people. In the meantime, it's the perfect size to provide tension relief to my pet hamster. Mr. Nippers really seems to like our massage sessions. And his wheel running has been much looser and easy-going since we started. Nice product!

I am not the best at flossing. I admit I do not do it nearly as much as I should. So having a very high quality toothbrush if very important. I have had Oral-B toothbrushs for quite some time. This is my favorite by far.First the power. It really gives your teeth a good thorough deep cleaning. My regularly used my old Oral-B but when I started with this one, my teeth/gums bled a little for around 4 to 5 days. So this one was 'working them out more'. The bleeding has gone away. I also like that the 30 second beeps (or 45 second beeps) instead of being all the same beep once at 30 second, twice at 60 seconds, and so on. There is also a more noticable pause allowing you to move the toothbrush.You also have the various settings. Deeo cleaniung is the same as regular (daily clean mode) just 45 second inteernvals.I haven't tried the whitening mode much as I really like daily clean or deep clean. My teeth feel great, and feel very clean. Where the toothbrush feels a little short for me is the stand and brush holder. They are cheap, not very space effective, and for me at least leave a lot of residue under the brush. The blue piece as far as I can tell is a travel coffin. Also a little on the cheap side.But those are minor compared to a very effective tooth cleaning tool. Yes I should floss more. But at least I know I am getting state of the art effective teeth cleaning every time I brush with this. Recommended.

I found the Oral-B Smartseries 4000 Rechargeable Toothbrush to be very comfortable in my hand, not too heavy, and very easy to manipulate in my mouth.  I also liked the various settings and often used the "Daily Clean" setting in which the toothbrush stops and beeps so that you get the proper amount of brushing in all 4 quadrants of your mouth.  As I have sensitive teeth, I also liked it that the toothbrush has a pressure sensor in order to keep me from over brushing.  My old toothbrush was theOral-B Professional Care 1000 Electric Toothbrushand the Smartseries 4000 obviously has many more options and advantages over this brush - but is also almost twice as expensive.Overall it's a great toothbrush, should $100 be within your budget to spend.

This one is definitely a step up from my cheaper Oral-B electric brush!  I like that it has an adjustable timer so I know how long to brush each "quadrant".  Very helpful.  I also like that it uses the same brush attachments as my now defunct/cheaper Oral-B so that I don't have to go out and buy a different brush attachment.  And I appreciate the travel case on those rare occasions that I am out of town.

I noticed that the battery in the Oral-B rechargeable toothbrush I've had for two years seems to be wearing down as it will start laboring even before the battery icon starts to blink, indicating it needs to be charged.  So, I decided to order a new one before my old one dies completely.  This is a little different as a red light blinks when you turn it on/off if the battery needs to be recharged.  However, it surprised me because when the  red light started blinking, the brushing was as vigorous as when I had just charged it.  My purchase also qualified for a rebate so am very pleased.JUST TO ALERT PEOPLE WHO SEND FOR THE REBATE - My order came in a brown cardboard box with a large adhesive label with the UPC code - I cut that off (in the bottom left-hand corner of the rebate form, it does say to do that) and sent it in with the rebate form and my invoice - all of that was returned to me with a form letter that stated that the UPC I submitted was "not valid" for the rebate offer!  I e-mailed Amazon and they were very nice and credited my card with the $20.  I don't understand what is wrong with the Oral-B people that they are saying I submitted an invalid UPC since that was the only thing on the box!!

We  have owned different Oral B power toothbrushes over the years with the latest being the 1000.  I have no complaints with the 1000 it works.When determining if one should purchase the 4000 or the 1000, one needs to look at the additional features the 4000 provides.If you have sensitive gums then you will need to purchase the 4000, there is a gentle mode.  Additionally, I would not recommend purchasing the floss action heads if you have sensitive gums.While there is an in handle display in the 4000, it is not a digital display.  It is a light next to the feature you are currently selectingI do not see any difference when using the polishing mode in the 4000, however, I do not have stains to determine if the feature works.The one annoyance for me with the 4000 is that I accidentally change the mode instead of turning off the brush from time to time because the button are close enough to do this.While I recommend the Oral B 4000, I would suggest using the 1000 if you do not have sensitive or stained teeth.

I've been using Oral-B electric toothbrushes for ten years.  Over the past year, I've been using an Oral-B 1000.  I definitely give the 1000 a thumbs up.  It's a big improvement over my old Excel 3D (which died after nine years of problem free use) in terms of motor speed and thoroughness of cleaning, plus it only cost me twenty bucks after a rebate.The 4000 seems to be the same as the 1000 except with a few more bells and whistles.  In basic brushing mode, I think both do an outstanding job.  The 4000 comes with a case (a nice touch), plus a place to put extra toothbrushes (can be useful if you have a family that all use an electric).  It also offers four cleaning modes, standard, deep, whitening, and sensitive cleaning.  Personally, all I need is standard mode.  The 4000 has a red light that goes on when you brush too hard.  Personally, I don't need that either.  Unlike the 1000, my 4000 came with a whitening brush, not the standard Oral B precision cleaning brush.  I prefer the standard precision cleaning brush and use it instead.If all you want is to do a great job cleaning your teeth in the morning and night, I'd say save money and get the 1000.  If you're the kind of person who likes to experiment with different cleaning modes and is very concerned about keeping your teeth as cosmetically white as can be, the 4000 might be worth the extra money.

I have had the earlier version of this Braun electric toothbrush for ten years. I have been pleased with the old one, and was happy to have the chance to get this newer version of this trusted tooth brush.First, both the old and new model do an exceptional job of cleaning your teeth. The small oscillating brush reaches those tough to get to problem areas like behind your rear teeth, and the inward side of all your teeth. Particularly, it really gets the back side of the front teeth clean, all the way to the gum line.The handle recharges by simply parking it in the base unit when not in use. There is nothing to plug in, it is inductively coupled, so just setting it on the base unit keeps it charged up.There are several things I like better about this unit, compared to the old one I have. The charging base has been redesigned, and looks nicer and more streamlined.The only thing I did not like as well about this one compared to the old one is that this new unit is louder than the old one. The old Braun unit was very quiet, and just had a pleasant humming sound. This is much more of a loud buzz. The reason I only gave this one four stars is because of the noise level.I feel this unit is well worth the money, and does an exceptional job of keeping all areas of your teeth clean. The little toothbrushes that fit on the unit last a long time, so the set up represents an excellent value overall.

Your teeth feel very smooth after ever session with the Oral-B Professional Care products. The question is which one to get? If you have sensitive gums, then I recommend the more expensive versions (3000 or higher) since they have sensitivity settings.  If you are sharing the device with others in your household who may not have the same gum sensitivity as yourself, again, I recommend version 3000 or higher since you may not fully understand others' sensitivity levels.  Plus the 3000 model (or higher) comes with a covered container for holding multiple brushes which will prevent the family from misplacing or swapping the heads.I own the Oral-B Professional Care 1000 Power Toothbrush and now the 4000. I was perfectly happy with the 1000, but I had the opportunity to acquire the 4000 so I thought why not? The 4000 though gives you different modes as well as a easily seen red light letting you know if you are pushing too hard. I didn't really need these bells and whistles, but they don't hurt. I've always thought that if this kind of tooth brush was painful then I either need to stop pushing so hard or buy softer heads.  That's just me, but I know others prefer having the feedback.It's hard for me to spend $100 plus on a toothbrush, so if money is a concern, I recommend the 1000 or the 3000. I don't really need those extra feedback lights, and often I have to change the 4000 to polish mode to get it to behave more like the 1000 (which is ideally what I want).  Note for me that the grip of the 1000/3000 feels better in the hand as well due to its smaller size compared to the 4000.I further recommend you order at least two different kinds of heads so you can figure out the style you like. The 4000 does come with one head, and this is the type that integrates some sort of rubber/plastic in addition to having bristles (e.g., FlossAction). I think I prefer the heads that are made up of only traditional bristles but heads with the added plastic do seem to polish more.Whether you get Braun or Phillips, do your teeth a favor and get a power toothbrush. Once you use these power toothbrushes, you'll never want to go back to the manual way.

I am a big fan of all of Oral-B's rechargeable toothbrushes. It's great to see different models accommodating different budgets. All of them do a great job and is way better than normal brushing alone.I currently have the Oral-B 1000 Professional Care and am very happy with it. When I got the 4000, I wondered if there would be a true difference between these two models. Does it really make a difference when cleaning and is it worth the extra bucks?I compared both the 1000 and the 4000 using the same exact brush head. Both gave an excellent clean, but I will have to admit, the 4000 gave a squeak better clean especially in areas that the 1000 seems to have trouble with.The 4000 looks, feels, and acts like a expensive dental machine. It has a light that goes on to warn you when you're using too much pressure. Every 15 seconds it will pause and do a *beep* (1, 2, 3, then 4 beeps) alerting you how close you're getting to the 2 minute mark. It also has different modes: Daily Clean, Sensitive, Whitening, Deep Clean. Daily Clean has been the only one we've really used.Th flossing brush head action works nicely. Not only do I feel it massaging my gums (which is something my hygienist recommends I do), I noticed that it also cut down much of the debris removed when I floss afterwards. It's doing something right.Other good things about the 4000 model is that it comes with a handy case for traveling and the stand has a storage rack to hold several toothbrush heads. This is a handy feature for either family use or keeping different style heads on hand.The 4000 is indeed the luxurious creme-de-la-creme of Oral-B electric toothbrushes. Is it worth the extra dollars? Yes, I think so. Everything on 4000 model is upgraded from the 1000 model with extra features added.The only potential drawback to buying any of the Oral B electric toothbrushes is the price of the replacement heads. The brush heads do a marvelous job, but they are not cheap.

We are a family of Oral B electronic tooth brush users. I have the 3000 and the kids each have a 1000. For the extra money the only thing I see that is worth any added expense is the carrying case. My husband works out of town and this is great for him to take the brush to/from work each week. Everything else is essentially the same as my 3000. Longer cleaning cycle means it beeps at 45 seconds and not 30. So what? You can figure that out on your own without paying an extra price tag. I say buy the 3000.

I would not want to brush my teeth manually after using this. Great at cleaning teeth, but be careful around the gum lines. Very basic with a push of a button and the rapid blinking light tells you when to charge it. It also auto shuts off in 2 mins. Would recommend to others and beats any deals out there so far.

Briefly, for a lot less than half the price, the Essence offers the essential benefits of the higher-end Sonicare products and it should be a good buy for both existing Sonicare users who don't care about paying a premium for a higher end models' bells and whistles and for new/undecided users who would like to try out Sonicare without spending a lot in the process. I am very happy with the product and, despite it lacking some of the higher end models features (see below) the Essence is going to replace my current Sonicare brush.Background: my records show that I've been using aPhilips Sonicare Advance 4100 Sonic Power Toothbrushsince May, 2008 and I used a Sonicare toothbrush before that which should qualify me as both a 'loyal customer' and, hopefully, a knowledgeable reviewer for the Essence.The Essence shares some key features with most other Sonicare products:* VIBRATION METHOD - Cleaning takes place because the brush head vibrates hundreds of times per second. This usually results in 'love at first try' or immediate rejection. It was instant love for me but my wife simply can't stand the vibration and she will never use a Sonicare product so... try before you buy. Philips will allow you to return the product for a full refund if you are not fully satisfied with your purchase.* REPLACEABLE HEADS - The most controversial aspect of Sonicare is the relatively pricey brush heads. They only last 3 months (4-6 months is possible but anything beyond that is not recommended) and they are not cheap whether you use a high end or a low end model.* INDUCTIVE CHARGING - You simply drop your toothbrush into the charger's cradle and it will charge without having to plug it in and there are not metal parts making contact. This is very convenient and very high-tech looking.* TWO-MINUTE TIMER - The Essence will stop running after 2 minutes which is the agreed-upon minimum time one should spend brushing teeth. Of course, you can start it again if the initial 2 minutes were not sufficient.* CORD WRAP - Like most Sonicare models, this one has one at the bottom of the charger to store any excess cord.* CARY CASE - The ESSENCE actually comes with a zippered pouch. It's better than nothing but not as protective as the Advance's solid plastic case.* MANUAL - It's English only and it discussing proper brushing techniques, product features, maintenance and simple troubleshooting.* 2 YEAR WARRANTY - on defects. Does not cover the brush heads, wear and tear, misuse or chips and scratches.And, these are the features that the Essence is missing when compared with the Advance which may or may not matter, depending on your needs. I am prefixing them with a '-' (minus).- QUADPACER - The Advance and other models give the user a signal every 30 seconds. This should help properly dividing brushing time between the 4 sections of your mouth (up/down, right/left).- BATTERY CHARGE INDICATOR - Higher end models would show through a string of small LEDs the battery's charge level. A nice to have feature but can easily live without it.- HARD CASE - see above. There is a soft one provided.Anyone who doesn't need the quadpacer feature could save some serious money by going with this less expensive model. When it comes to tooth-brushing, the Essence and the Advance perform very much the same. The Advance body is slightly slimmer and shorter but I'm comparing today's Essence with the Advance of over 4 years ago so they might be the same now.Overall, I am very pleased with both models but if I had to pick between the 'basic' Essence and the more feature-rich and a lot more expensive Advance today I'd definitely go with the more affordable Essence and use the savings to buy my a couple of years worth of brush heads.

If you were to poll the faculty, students, and staff at our dental school (~1000), you'd find the vast majority using Sonicare with a manual toothbrush next in line.I've been using Sonicare for almost two decades with excellent success.  My healthcare is so good that I choose a hygienist first and then use whatever doctor she works for to confirm that I don't need any work.  Before I started using Sonicare I had receding gum lines and carpel tunnel from brushing too hard.  No problem with either now.My Sonicare of choice is the HX6972/10 FlexCare Plus because I love the idea of tucking the head away in a sanitizer.  But a sanitizer is not necessary if you protect the head from exposure to germs (like having your toothbrush next to the john) or use another method of sanitizing the head (mouthwash will do).This brush is simpler in a good way.  The more expensive (twice as expensive) Flexcare has a bunch of buttons on the handle to change the speed.  But no one changes the speed after they get used to using it and I keep accidentally pushing them.This brush has one button - yay!  Because of it's simplicity, I'm giving it to one of the three-year-old twins.  The other one would clean the floor with it.The heads are cheap enough (watch for sales and check Sams Club) and will last longer than you should be using them.  Regardless of what you pay for the brush, it'll be worth the price because of improved dental and, consequentially, medical health.I only had one brush body go bad on me and Philips replaced it for free (be sure to register the brush).Nothing bad to say about these brushes or the company.

My dentist recommended this and I ordered it online since it was so much cheaper. I got it and started using it about 3 months before my next check up. My dentist told me he could tell the difference before I even told him I had gotten it. Less hard plaque, which I tend to build up quite quickly. It was hard to get used to at first. It feels weird, but it even says in the directions that it will feel odd at first and your gums get used to it. Mine did and now I love love love it.
This Phillips Sonicare HX5610/30 Essence 5600 Rechargeable Electric Toothbrush is a replacement.  Great quality, I am very pleased.Thank you

We have been using the Sonicare basic model for almost 1o years now and loving it.  My spouse inherited a genetic tendency towards recedinggums and gums thatbleed easily.  Using the Sonicare basic model regularly radically improved her dental health and over time eliminated any new cavities.  This NEW model, the Sonicare essence has a few added features that we like an awful lot and will likely gradually swith out all our Sonicares over to when they quit. The last time my wife's Sonicare basic model quit, we could really afford to buy a new one but she was willing to do without to have her "wonder Toothbrush" back.  We immediately ordered another one from PRIME and in 2 days, she was again a happy woman.Some people will tell you there's no real difference and I was one of those for a couple of years but just comparing the dental bills made me a believer.  If you use the Sonicare Essence and its new features, you'll likely become as attached to as we are to the brand in general.  The new features are:* A ramp-up mode that allows you to slowly, over time, increase the speed until you are used to the full action of the brush - GREAT for sensitive gums* A better and smaller carrying case for travel and a thinner, lighter clamshell storage case for the bathroom.  You can still use your old blue one if you want - it fits.* Just like the other models, the timer tells you when to stop but has an added feature mode for cleaning deep periodontal pockets if you have a need for such cleanings - see instructions* Same easy charging station - wipes clean easily* Angled Bursh head with a redesigned set of bristles that seem to get at the back of your molars better and also gets under bridgework easier* Better grips on the sides - not as slippery when wet!The Instruction Booklet is something you should KEEP IN A SAFE PLACE because it explains everything that could go wrong.  The fixes are easy and well-explained but misplacing that booklet could be a problem.  keep it with your other imnportant instruction manuals.The potential downsides:*If you replace the brusheahds every 3 months as recommended, it can be expensive but Amazon has a Subscribe & Save Program for that and twice we've gotten an add-on money saving coupon* You need to start the bursh AFTER it's in your mouth or toothpaste will paint every surface in your bathroom!I can see the ram-up feature being useful to new-timers but for me, I turned it off after a couple of uses.  You may accidentally turn that on but the Booklet explains how to get it back to regular cleaning mode should that happen.In general, I can tell you from experience than even modest improvements to the design are worth trying out since the basic design is so much better than a manual toothbrush or any other electric I have ever tried and I've tried about 6 different brands.  The power base on my original failed after 8 years of use and I didn't mind buying an entire new brush just to get the base since that gave me a back-up brush handle.  The item is easily cleaned but be sure and read the instructions for that before you begin.  The person most impressed with the Soniccare technology was our family dentist who is very "old School" believing that a manual brush and a  box of floss will do it all for you.  He's now selling Soniccares in his office.  Honestly, this ONE change in your oral care routine could potentially save you the loss odf teeth and tens of thousands of dollars over your lifetime.  Use the theing every day, floss regulalry and maybe add a Water Pik routine once a week for deep cleaning and you won't be having major mouth restorations.  Even my wife, who is prone to gum disease and fragile tooth enamel due to heredity has seen amazing improvements.  We've been fighting over the new Brush handle and have already bought some extra brush tips so we can each use the newer model.  We will all eventually switch out to upgrade to this newer model.  They made an already excellent product that much better!

Many years ago it was recommened to me i use Sonic Care  i started and stillto this day i use this product. It has saved my teeth from more dentist vists.I am 100% in support of using this product.I use it everyday. This purchase is a replacement of my old one.

I have been using Sonicare products for almost 20 years and have never been disappointed by the results.This particular model comes in at a very reasonable price point.  The head is used on several models so that it will be easy to locate and purchase.There are two features on this particular model that I don't have on any of the previous models I've owned.  One is an easy-start option that gradually helps your gums adjust to using the product.  This is a nice addition for those new to the brand.The second feature is a soft grip handle.  Not necessary but increases comfort  and decreases the chances of the handle slipping if you're hands are a bit wet when brushing your teeth.I always appreciated the 2-minute timer option and this model has that as well.My single grip about Sonicare products is that it is necessary to remove the brush so that nothing drips into the top of the handle base.  Guck will gather and it's just nasty and difficult to clean.  I use rubbing alcohol and cotton swabs.  Just something to be aware of if you are new to the brand.An excellent product.  I highly recommend this!!

I've used Sonicare electric toothbrushes for many, many years.  This Sonicare Essence 5600/CleanCare is similar in shape and type of brush attachment to our first electric toothbrush, part of the Elite series and purchased years ago.  If you still have one of those old ones (and you might because one of ours still works), it's time to trade it out for this one.This Essence 5600/CleanCare has more motion, faster motion and a more "powerful" motion and does a much better job of cleaning because of that.  In shape, it is a little shorter and wider than the old Elite, but the weight is about the same.  The brush head still breaks apart for cleaning and still has the little magnets that work to produce the movement.  Something inside and out of sight, though, definitely gives it more "oomph".About 6 months ago the rubber button on one of our Elites deteriorated, tore and broke.  We replaced it with the Sonicare 700 Series Healthy White.  The brush head is simpler and the handle is narrower than both the Essence and the Elite.  The motion is better than the old Elite, but not as great as this new Essence.  While I like the brush head and the handle of the 700 Series much better (because it is more slender) than the Essence, the brushing motion of the Essence is so much better that I recommend the Essence for performance.  The Essence cleaned my teeth better--so much better, that I could feel the difference with my tongue.Other differences I've noticed:  The charging light is now located behind the rubber on/off button, and it is quite bright.  The Essence still has the feature of the soft rubber coating to help with hand-gripping.  You can get the rest of the particulars from reading the product page carefully.I highly recommend this new Essence/CleanCare:  I love the brushing motion and power; I like the simplicity of one button; I like the simple style of the charger base and the way you can hide the cord in the base, and I can live with the less slender base (compared to the 700 Series).Word to the wise:  If you buy a Sonicare toothbrush, treat the rubber on/off button with respect, (i.e. don't dig into it with your fingernail, don't push harder than you really have to, don't stick it with anything), because it is the most vulnerable part of the toothbrush.  I'm not saying that it is excessively delicate, but it is the most "used" part other than the replaceable brush head.
